entries.short_definition: The FAA regulation in Title 14 of the Code of Federal Regulations that prescribes procedures for certificating civil aircraft, engines, propellers, and articles in the United States.
entries.full_definition:
Part 21 of Title 14 of the Code of Federal Regulations, titled Certification Procedures for Products and Articles, sets the procedural framework under which the Federal Aviation Administration approves the design, production, and airworthiness of civil aviation products. Codified across Subparts A through P (sections 21.1 through 21.700), it governs how an applicant moves from a paper design to a flying article that the FAA recognizes as safe to operate. The companion airworthiness standards live in Parts 23, 25, 27, 29, 33, and 35; Part 21 is the procedural spine that binds them together (eCFR, 14 CFR Part 21).

A Type Certificate, issued under Subpart B and specifically section 21.21, approves the type design of an aircraft, engine, or propeller after the FAA finds that the design meets the applicable airworthiness, noise, fuel venting, and emissions standards, and that no feature makes the product unsafe for its category. Changes to an approved design follow Subpart D, and a third party seeking to modify a certificated product applies for a Supplemental Type Certificate under Subpart E. A Production Certificate, issued under Subpart G (sections 21.131 through 21.150), authorizes a holder to manufacture conforming articles under an approved quality system. An Airworthiness Certificate, issued under Subpart H (sections 21.171 through 21.199), is the per-aircraft document that authorizes operation; standard, special, restricted, and experimental categories all flow from this subpart (Cornell Legal Information Institute, 14 CFR Part 21).

Section 21.16 is the workhorse provision for novel vehicles. When the FAA finds that the airworthiness regulations do not contain adequate or appropriate safety standards because of a novel or unusual design feature, the agency issues Special Conditions that prescribe additional standards the applicant must meet. Special Conditions have been used for fly-by-wire transports, lithium-ion batteries on the Boeing 787, and powered-lift configurations such as the Joby JAS4-1, and are formally published in the Federal Register. The applicant and FAA also agree on Means of Compliance: the documented tests, analyses, and inspections that demonstrate each requirement is satisfied, captured in a certification plan negotiated early in the program.

Enforcement and surveillance run on parallel tracks. A Letter of Investigation, issued by an FAA inspector or aviation safety inspector, formally notifies a certificate holder that the agency is examining a possible regulatory violation and invites a written response before any sanction is proposed under 14 CFR Part 13. Part 21 also reaches into continued airworthiness through section 21.3 reporting of failures, malfunctions, and defects, and into international acceptance through Subpart L export airworthiness approvals.

entries.short_definition: FAA airworthiness standards for normal category airplanes up to 19 seats, restructured in 2017 around performance-based rules and industry consensus standards as accepted means of compliance.
entries.full_definition:
Title 14 of the Code of Federal Regulations, Part 23, sets the airworthiness standards the FAA applies when issuing type certificates for normal category airplanes with a maximum seating capacity of 19 or fewer passengers and a maximum takeoff weight of 19,000 pounds or less. It is the small-airplane counterpart to Part 25, which governs transport category aircraft, and it applies to powered fixed-wing designs ranging from light singles to business jets and, increasingly, to electric and hybrid configurations being certified under the same framework.

Amendment 23-64, published in the Federal Register on December 30, 2016 and effective August 30, 2017, retired roughly four decades of prescriptive design rules and replaced them with high-level safety objectives. The reorganized part is divided into seven subparts: A General (sections 23.2000-2015), B Flight (23.2100-2165), C Structures (23.2200-2270), D Design and Construction (23.2300-2335), E Powerplant (23.2400-2440), F Equipment (23.2500-2550), and G Flightcrew Interface and Other Information (23.2600-2620). Section 23.2005 establishes four certification levels keyed to maximum passenger seating: Level 1 for 0 to 1 passengers, Level 2 for 2 to 6, Level 3 for 7 to 9, and Level 4 for 10 to 19. Each level is further divided into low-speed and high-speed performance categories, with the threshold set at V_NO or V_MO of 250 KCAS and M_MO of 0.6. This four-by-two matrix lets the FAA scale rigor to the risk a given design actually presents.

Because the rule text is intentionally performance-based, detailed technical criteria live in industry consensus standards that the FAA accepts as means of compliance. ASTM Committee F44 on General Aviation Aircraft develops the primary set, anchored by ASTM F3264, Standard Specification for Normal Category Aeroplanes Certification. The FAA's acceptance process is defined in AC 23.2010-1 and tracked on the Part 23 Accepted Means of Compliance page, with the current list covering dozens of MOCs across F44 standards. Companion guidance, including AC 23-21 on compliance checklists for major alterations and the Amendment 23-64 Implementation Procedures Guide, walks applicants through showing compliance under the new structure. The FAA notes that novel architectures such as eVTOL or unmanned aircraft typically fall outside the accepted MOCs and require applicant-proposed alternatives or special conditions issued under Part 21.

entries.short_definition: Federal airworthiness standards governing the type certification of transport category airplanes, the design rulebook for jets and large turboprops operated in U.S. civil aviation.
entries.full_definition:
14 CFR Part 25, "Airworthiness Standards: Transport Category Airplanes," is the section of Title 14 of the Code of Federal Regulations that prescribes the design and construction requirements an applicant must meet to obtain a type certificate, or a change to a type certificate, for a transport category airplane. Section 25.1 states the applicability directly: the part applies to airplanes certificated under the transport category, and each applicant proceeding under Part 21 must show compliance with the applicable requirements. The transport category itself, as defined in Part 21 and the certification procedures, captures multi-engine jets with 10 or more seats or a maximum takeoff weight greater than 12,500 pounds, and propeller-driven airplanes with more than 19 seats or a maximum takeoff weight greater than 19,000 pounds. Aircraft such as the Boeing 737 and 787 families, the Airbus A320 and A350 families, and large turboprops including the ATR 72 are all certificated to Part 25.

Part 25 was added to 14 CFR effective February 1, 1965, replacing Part 4b of the Civil Air Regulations and recodifying Special Civil Air Regulation SR 422B, which had governed turbine-powered transports. Its structure has remained stable since: Subpart A (General, sections 25.1 through 25.5), Subpart B (Flight, 25.21 through 25.255), Subpart C (Structure, 25.301 through 25.581), Subpart D (Design and Construction, 25.601 through 25.899), Subpart E (Powerplant, 25.901 through 25.1207), Subpart F (Equipment, 25.1301 through 25.1461), Subpart G (Operating Limitations and Information, 25.1501 through 25.1587), Subpart H (Electrical Wiring Interconnection Systems, 25.1701 through 25.1733), and Subpart I (Special Federal Aviation Regulations, 25.1801). A series of appendices, including Appendix D on continuous turbulence and Appendix K on extended operations, carry specialized criteria referenced by the substantive sections.

The framework is amendment-numbered rather than rewritten: each rulemaking adds an amendment to the relevant sections and the certification basis for a given program is frozen at the amendment level in force on the application date, subject to the changed-product rule in Part 21.101. Recent amendments include Amendment 25-148, published at 87 FR 75710 on December 9, 2022, with a correction at 88 FR 2813 on January 18, 2023, and the 2024 rulemaking on equipment, systems, and network information security protection finalized at 89 FR 67428 on August 21, 2024. Where novel features fall outside the existing rules, the FAA imposes special conditions under 14 CFR 21.16; where the rule is met by other means, it grants equivalent level of safety findings. Together with Part 21 procedures and the Part 26 continued operational safety rules, Part 25 forms the backbone of transport airplane certification in the United States, and its text is harmonized with EASA CS-25 across most subparts. The eCFR maintains the controlling current version.

entries.short_definition: Design-specific airworthiness regulations the FAA issues under 14 CFR 21.16 when existing standards lack adequate or appropriate safety requirements for a novel or unusual feature.
entries.full_definition:
A special condition is a binding airworthiness regulation that applies to one particular type design. Under 14 CFR 21.16, the FAA prescribes special conditions when it finds that the airworthiness regulations of subchapter C do not contain adequate or appropriate safety standards for an aircraft, aircraft engine, or propeller because of a novel or unusual design feature. The special conditions are issued in accordance with the rulemaking procedures of 14 CFR part 11 and must contain the safety standards the FAA finds necessary to establish a level of safety equivalent to that established by the existing regulations. They are published in the Federal Register, attached to the type certificate, and become part of the certification basis for that design.

In practice, special conditions are how the FAA closes the gap when a project outruns the existing parts. Parts 23, 25, 27, 29, 33, and 35 were written around piston, turbine, and conventional airframe configurations, and contain little or nothing on high-voltage batteries, distributed electric propulsion, tilting rotors, or full hybrid-electric architectures. For powered-lift eVTOL programs the FAA has used 14 CFR 21.17(b) special class procedures to construct a certification basis out of pieces of parts 23, 25, 27, and 29 plus design-specific criteria, as it did for the Joby Aero Model JAS4-1 with airworthiness criteria effective April 8, 2024. For novel propulsion units it has gone directly to 21.16, issuing special conditions for the Safran Electric and Power Model ENGINeUS 100A1 electric engine in late 2024 because part 33 was written for reciprocating and turbine engines and does not cover electric motors, motor controllers, or high-voltage power electronics.

A typical special conditions package is narrow and prescriptive. It cites the applicable type certification application, identifies the novel feature (lithium-ion battery installation, hybrid powerplant, fly-by-wire flight controls, composite primary structure, high-energy rotors), and writes new numbered requirements that the applicant must show compliance with alongside the underlying part. The FAA publishes a notice of proposed special conditions, takes comment, and then issues the final special conditions; they apply only to the named model and any later derivative the FAA designates. Special conditions sit alongside, not in place of, the type certificate under 14 CFR 21.21, and they are distinct from equivalent level of safety findings, exemptions under part 11, and the broader special class path of 21.17(b).

entries.short_definition: An FAA design approval issued under 14 CFR 21 Subpart B that certifies an aircraft, engine, or propeller type design meets the applicable airworthiness and environmental requirements.
entries.full_definition:
A type certificate is the FAA's approval of a specific aircraft, aircraft engine, or propeller type design under 14 CFR Part 21 Subpart B. The TC documents that the type design complies with the applicable airworthiness, noise, fuel venting, exhaust emission, and fuel efficiency requirements of 14 CFR, and it forms the legal basis for any subsequent production approval or airworthiness certificate issued for an individual article built to that design.

Under 14 CFR 21.15, an applicant submits a type certificate application on a form prescribed by the FAA, accompanied for aircraft by a three-view drawing and available preliminary basic data, and for engines by a description of design features, operating characteristics, and proposed operating limitations. The certification basis is fixed by 14 CFR 21.17: the applicant must show compliance with the requirements of Parts 23, 25, 27, 29, 31, 33, 35, and 36 that are effective on the date of application, plus any special conditions issued under 14 CFR 21.16 to address novel or unusual design features. An application is effective for 5 years for transport-category aircraft and 3 years for any other type certificate; if certification is not completed within that window, the applicant must refile or extend by selecting a later effective date for the certification basis under 14 CFR 21.17(d).

Before any FAA inspection or flight test, 14 CFR 21.33 requires the applicant to conduct conformity inspections demonstrating that materials, parts, manufacturing processes, construction, and assembly all conform to the type design, and to allow the FAA to make any inspection and ground or flight test necessary to determine compliance. Flight testing required under 14 CFR 21.35 includes the operational and functional flight tests the FAA finds necessary, conducted after the airframe, engine, and propeller combination has been shown to be reliable and functioning properly.

When the Administrator finds the type design meets the applicable requirements and that no feature or characteristic of the aircraft makes it unsafe for the category sought, the FAA issues the type certificate under 14 CFR 21.21. Per 14 CFR 21.41, the type certificate is deemed to include the type design, the operating limitations, the type certificate data sheet (TCDS), the applicable regulations with which the FAA records compliance, and any other conditions or limitations prescribed for the product. The TC is the predicate document for production certificates under Subpart G and standard airworthiness certificates under Subpart H.

entries.short_definition: 10 CFR Part 50 is the NRC's legacy two-step reactor licensing rule, issuing a construction permit and then a separate operating license for production and utilization facilities.
entries.full_definition:
Title 10, Code of Federal Regulations, Part 50, "Domestic Licensing of Production and Utilization Facilities," is the foundational Nuclear Regulatory Commission rule for civilian reactor licensing, promulgated under the Atomic Energy Act of 1954 and Title II of the Energy Reorganization Act of 1974. Part 50 establishes the legacy two-step process under which essentially every currently operating US commercial power reactor was licensed: first a construction permit (CP) authorizing the applicant to build but not operate, and then, after construction is substantially complete, a separate operating license (OL) authorizing fuel load and power operation. Class 103 commercial licenses, held by all electricity-generating reactor facilities today, are issued under this framework (nrc.gov, Backgrounder on Nuclear Power Plant Licensing Process).

The technical content of a Part 50 application is defined by 10 CFR 50.34. Section 50.34(a) requires a Preliminary Safety Analysis Report (PSAR) at the CP stage, describing the preliminary design, site characteristics, principal design criteria, and preliminary safety analysis. Section 50.34(b) then requires a Final Safety Analysis Report (FSAR) at the OL stage, reflecting the as-built final design, technical specifications, and accident analyses. The key practical distinction from the one-step combined license under 10 CFR Part 52 is that Part 50 lets a utility begin construction with preliminary, not finalized, design information.

Part 50 carries the substantive safety appendices that anchor the wider US reactor regulatory regime. Appendix A, "General Design Criteria for Nuclear Power Plants," issued as a final rule on February 20, 1971, sets the high-level light-water reactor design requirements, beginning with Criterion 1 on quality standards and records. Appendix B, "Quality Assurance Criteria for Nuclear Power Plants and Fuel Reprocessing Plants," prescribes the eighteen QA criteria for safety-related structures, systems, and components. Appendix R, "Fire Protection Program for Nuclear Power Facilities Operating Prior to January 1, 1979," imposes prescriptive fire protection requirements on pre-1979 plants, including post-fire safe-shutdown separation provisions; later plants are governed by their license conditions and NFPA 805 (nrc.gov, Fire Protection Regulations in 10 CFR Part 50). Other notable appendices include Appendix I (ALARA effluent design objectives) and Appendix N (standardized multi-site CPs).

In practice, the entire operating US fleet, including units such as Vogtle 1 and 2, Palo Verde, and Diablo Canyon, holds Part 50 CPs and OLs; only the newer Vogtle 3 and 4 reactors were licensed under Part 52. License renewals beyond the initial 40-year term are handled under 10 CFR Part 54, but the underlying license remains a Part 50 instrument.

entries.short_definition: The U.S. NRC regulation, codified in 1989, that authorises a one-step Combined Licence for nuclear power plants alongside Early Site Permits and Standard Design Certifications.
entries.full_definition:
10 CFR Part 52, "Licenses, Certifications, and Approvals for Nuclear Power Plants," is the U.S. Nuclear Regulatory Commission rule that consolidates construction and operating authorisation into a single proceeding. The NRC promulgated the original Part 52 on 18 April 1989 to add predictability to reactor licensing, and substantially revised it in 2007 to align with modern reactor designs. It runs in parallel with the older two-step process under 10 CFR Part 50, and applicants today generally elect Part 52 for new commercial reactors (nrc.gov).

Part 52 offers three principal instruments. Subpart A governs Early Site Permits (ESP), which approve a site's suitability under §§ 52.17 through 52.39 without committing to a specific reactor design and remain valid for 10 to 20 years. Subpart B governs Standard Design Certifications (DC), issued by Commission rulemaking as an appendix to Part 52; certified designs to date include the ABWR (Appendix A), System 80+ (Appendix B), AP600 (Appendix C), AP1000 (Appendix D), ESBWR (Appendix E), APR1400 (Appendix F), and the NuScale small modular reactor (Appendix G, published in the Federal Register on 19 January 2023 and effective 21 February 2023). Subpart C governs the Combined Licence (COL) itself, a combined construction permit and operating licence under § 52.97 that authorises both build and operation subject to verification (nrc.gov).

The verification mechanism is the Inspections, Tests, Analyses, and Acceptance Criteria, or ITAAC, required by § 52.97(b) and § 52.99. ITAAC define the objective tests a licensee must complete before fuel load; most are inherited from the referenced Design Certification, with the remainder added as site-specific commitments in the COL. Section 52.103(g) requires the Commission to find, before operation, that the ITAAC acceptance criteria have been met, a determination that triggers a window for hearing requests under § 52.103(a) and (b) (nrc.gov).

The first COLs ever issued under Part 52 went to Southern Nuclear for Vogtle Units 3 and 4 in February 2012, referencing the AP1000 certified design in Appendix D; Unit 3 entered commercial operation on 31 July 2023 and Unit 4 on 29 April 2024. The NuScale rulemaking in Appendix G, certifying a 50 MWe module, is the first Part 52 design certification for a small modular reactor and provides the design that future COL applicants may incorporate by reference (nrc.gov).

entries.short_definition: 10 CFR Part 53 is the NRC's risk-informed, technology-inclusive, performance-based licensing framework for commercial nuclear plants, offered as an optional alternative to Parts 50 and 52.
entries.full_definition:
10 CFR Part 53, formally titled "Risk-Informed, Technology-Inclusive Regulatory Framework for Commercial Nuclear Plants," is a new optional licensing pathway codified by the Nuclear Regulatory Commission for advanced and conventional reactor designs. The Commission approved the final rule on March 26, 2026, the Federal Register published it on March 30, 2026, and it became effective on April 29, 2026, roughly 20 months ahead of the December 31, 2027 deadline that Congress imposed in section 103(a)(4) of the Nuclear Energy Innovation and Modernization Act of 2019 (NEIMA), as reinforced by the ADVANCE Act of 2024. Rather than prescribing light-water-reactor-specific requirements, Part 53 sets safety objectives and lets applicants demonstrate compliance using a probabilistic risk assessment (PRA), other systematic risk evaluations, or a combination, with consensus codes and standards graded to the safety significance of each structure, system, and component (nrc.gov).

The rule is organised into roughly a dozen lettered subparts. Subpart A sets general provisions, Subpart B contains the safety requirements (including the quantitative health objectives that anchor the framework), and Subpart C governs design and analysis. Subpart D, "Siting Requirements," obliges applicants to evaluate external hazards such as geologic and seismic conditions, population factors, and emergency and security logistics, and to show that the site does not undermine Subpart B's safety criteria. A distinctive feature is that Subpart D permits siting in higher-density areas, including locations with more than roughly 25,000 nearby residents, provided the applicant demonstrates that societal risk is acceptable relative to societal benefits. Subparts E through K cover construction and manufacturing, operations, decommissioning, licensing processes, license maintenance, reporting, and quality assurance, and a later block of subparts (commonly cited as N through U) houses an alternative evaluation framework for risk insights (ecfr.gov).

Key differences from Parts 50 and 52 are structural rather than only procedural. Parts 50 and 52 retain a largely deterministic, prescriptive safety architecture (two-step construction permit and operating licence under Part 50; combined licence, design certification, and early site permit under Part 52). Part 53 keeps those licence vehicles but reorients them around performance criteria, mandates plant-specific PRAs, narrows design-basis accident analysis to the selection and functional criteria for safety-related SSCs, omits the Part 50.150 aircraft impact assessment, allows factory fuel loading for transportable reactors, and explicitly accommodates non-light-water technologies without bespoke exemptions. The detailed siting metrics referenced in industry summaries, including any design-basis ground motion and seismic design categorisation provisions, are implemented through Subpart D together with the accompanying regulatory guides; the precise section numbers and terminology should be confirmed against the codified text in the eCFR (federalregister.gov).

entries.short_definition: ARCAP is the U.S. NRC project that produced technology-inclusive, risk-informed guidance covering the non-safety-analysis content of advanced reactor applications under 10 CFR Parts 50, 52, and 53.
entries.full_definition:
The Advanced Reactor Content of Application Project (ARCAP) is the U.S. Nuclear Regulatory Commission staff effort to develop technology-inclusive, risk-informed, and performance-based guidance on the content of license applications for non-light-water reactors. ARCAP builds on the Licensing Modernization Project (NEI 18-04) and is the regulator-side counterpart to the industry-led Technology-Inclusive Content of Application Project (TICAP), which is documented in NEI 21-07. With respect to the safety analysis report, TICAP guidance addresses most of Chapters 1 through 8, while ARCAP guidance addresses Chapters 9 through 12 and the non-SAR portions of an application. The framework supports applications for combined licenses, construction permits, operating licenses, design certifications, standard design approvals, and manufacturing licenses under 10 CFR Part 50 and Part 52, and informs the 10 CFR Part 53 rulemaking.

ARCAP was implemented through three principal draft regulatory guides. DG-1383, published in the Federal Register on September 30, 2021, endorsed the 2019 Edition of ASME Boiler and Pressure Vessel Code Section XI, Division 2, "Requirements for Reliability and Integrity Management (RIM) Programs," with conditions for non-LWR application. It was finalized as Regulatory Guide 1.246 on November 3, 2022. DG-1413 proposed new Regulatory Guide 1.254, "Technology-Inclusive Identification of Licensing Events for Commercial Nuclear Plants," providing a method to identify event sequences from a probabilistic risk assessment and to classify them as anticipated operational occurrences, design-basis events, or beyond-design-basis events. DG-1414 is the companion guide for selecting the postulated bounding event whose consequence analysis informs siting, dose, and design-basis decisions, taking the licensing event set from DG-1413 as input.

The TICAP side of the framework was finalized as Regulatory Guide 1.253, Revision 0, issued April 3, 2024 (originally DG-1404), which endorses NEI 21-07, Revision 1, "Technology Inclusive Guidance for Non-Light Water Reactors Safety Analysis Report Content for Applicants Utilizing NEI 18-04 Methodology." Regulatory Guide 1.254, Revision 0, was issued in October 2024. Together, the ARCAP and TICAP guides give a near-term non-LWR applicant a complete, NRC-endorsed roadmap from PRA-derived event selection through SAR chapter content, replacing reliance on the LWR-centric Regulatory Guide 1.206 for technology-specific portions of an application.

entries.short_definition: A Combined License issued under 10 CFR 52 Subpart C is a single NRC authorization to both construct and operate a nuclear power facility, contingent on satisfying ITAAC.
entries.full_definition:
A Combined License, or COL, is the one-step construction-and-operating authorization established by 10 CFR Part 52, Subpart C. It replaces the sequential Part 50 path of separate construction permit and operating license with a single license issued before construction begins. A COL applicant typically references a certified standard design under Subpart B and may reference an early site permit under Subpart A, allowing the NRC to resolve siting, design, and operational issues in one proceeding and reducing the prospect of late-stage relitigation once capital is committed.

Under 10 CFR 52.97, the Commission may issue a COL only after a mandatory hearing and an ACRS report, and only on findings that applicable Atomic Energy Act and NRC requirements are met, that there is reasonable assurance the facility will be constructed and operated in conformity with the license, that the applicant is technically and financially qualified, and that issuance is consistent with common defense, security, and public health and safety, together with the environmental findings required by 10 CFR Part 51. Section 52.97 also permits the Commission to find at issuance that specific acceptance criteria in ITAAC drawn from a referenced early site permit or design certification have already been met, in which case those criteria are excluded from the license and no further 52.103(g) finding is required for them.

The license itself incorporates Inspections, Tests, Analyses, and Acceptance Criteria, the ITAAC, which the licensee must satisfy during construction to demonstrate that the as-built plant conforms to the approved design and site parameters. Operation is gated by 10 CFR 52.103(g): the licensee may not load fuel or operate until the Commission finds that the acceptance criteria in the COL are met, other than those already resolved at issuance under 52.97(a)(2). The Commission publishes notice of its intended 52.103(g) finding and provides for limited hearings on contentions that specific ITAAC have not been met.

The first COLs issued under this framework were Vogtle Electric Generating Plant Units 3 and 4 (NPF-91 and NPF-92), granted to Southern Nuclear Operating Company on February 10, 2012 for two AP1000 units in Georgia; Vogtle 3 entered commercial operation in 2023 and Vogtle 4 in 2024. The NRC issued COLs NPF-93 and NPF-94 for V.C. Summer Units 2 and 3 to South Carolina Electric and Gas on March 30, 2012; those licenses were terminated on March 6, 2019 after construction was abandoned.

entries.short_definition: A U.S. Nuclear Regulatory Commission approval under 10 CFR 52 Subpart A that resolves site safety, environmental, and emergency planning issues for one or more future nuclear power reactors, independent of design.
entries.full_definition:
An early site permit (ESP) is a partial Commission approval issued under 10 CFR Part 52, Subpart A (sections 52.1 through 52.39) that resolves site-related safety, environmental, and emergency planning questions for a proposed nuclear power reactor site before, and decoupled from, the choice of reactor design or any decision to build. Per 10 CFR 52.17, the applicant files a Site Safety Analysis Report (SSAR) describing the number, type, and thermal power level of the facilities, or a plant parameter envelope (PPE) bounding a range of possible designs, together with anticipated radiological and thermal effluents, cooling system configurations, seismic and meteorological characterization, exclusion area boundaries, and a discussion of contacts with federal, state, and local agencies with emergency planning responsibilities.

Under 10 CFR 52.33, an ESP is valid for not less than 10 nor more than 20 years from issuance, and each renewal runs for an additional 10 to 20 years plus any time remaining on the original permit. An ESP for which a timely renewal application has been filed remains in effect pending Commission action. The principal value of the permit comes from 10 CFR 52.39, which makes site-suitability findings final and bars relitigation in any subsequent construction permit or combined license (COL) proceeding that references the ESP, except on narrow grounds such as whether the proposed reactor fits the approved site parameters, whether a variance is warranted, or whether new emergency-planning information materially alters prior conclusions. The mechanism therefore bankrolls site characterization work, NEPA review, and public participation, letting a developer accumulate hundreds of millions of dollars of regulatory equity that can be referenced later by a COL applicant under 10 CFR 52.79.

Six ESPs have been issued by the NRC. Clinton (ESP-001, March 15, 2007), Grand Gulf (ESP-002, April 5, 2007), and North Anna (ESP-003, November 27, 2007) were the first wave, all using surrogate plant parameter envelopes. Vogtle Units 3 and 4 (ESP-004, August 26, 2009) was the first ESP tied to a specific design, the Westinghouse AP1000, and was issued together with a limited work authorization. The PSEG Site permit on Artificial Island, New Jersey (ESP-005, May 5, 2016) was the first ESP issued without a co-pending COL application. Clinch River (ESP-006, December 19, 2019) was the first ESP for small modular reactors, approving up to 800 MWe of SMR capacity at the Tennessee Valley Authority site under a PPE approach. Grand Gulf filed a renewal application that the NRC docketed in January 2026, and Clinton's renewal proceeding was noticed in April 2025.

entries.short_definition: IAEA Specific Safety Requirements No. SSR-2/1 establishes design requirements for civilian land-based stationary nuclear power plants, currently in Revision 1 published in 2016.
entries.full_definition:
IAEA SSR-2/1, "Safety of Nuclear Power Plants: Design," is the principal document in the IAEA Safety Standards Series that sets out the specific safety requirements applicable to the design of civilian land-based stationary nuclear power plants. It elaborates the safety objective, fundamental safety principles, and design concepts established in the IAEA Safety Fundamentals (SF-1) and translates them into a structured set of requirements that cover the application of the management system to design, the use of defence in depth, plant states (including design basis accidents and design extension conditions), safety classification, the safety functions of structures, systems and components, and the design of specific plant systems such as the reactor core, reactor coolant, containment, and instrumentation and control (IAEA).

The current edition is SSR-2/1 (Rev. 1), approved by the IAEA Board of Governors and published in 2016, superseding the original 2012 issuance and the earlier NS-R-1 (2000). The revision was driven primarily by lessons learned from the 2011 Fukushima Daiichi accident: it strengthens provisions for prevention of severe accidents and mitigation of their consequences, formalizes the treatment of design extension conditions (notably in Requirement 20), and tightens requirements on independence of safety systems, protection against external hazards including site-specific natural events, and avoidance of long-term off-site contamination. The document is structured around numbered Requirements organized into sections covering application of the management system, the principal technical requirements, general plant design, and design of specific plant systems (IAEA).

IAEA safety standards, including SSR-2/1, are not legally binding on Member States. They are issued under the IAEA Statute and serve as a reference that States may adopt, in whole or in part, when establishing or revising their national regulatory frameworks. National regulators commonly use SSR-2/1 to benchmark domestic design rules: in the United States, NRC requirements in 10 CFR Part 50 Appendix A (General Design Criteria) and the licensing pathways in 10 CFR Parts 52 and 53 cover similar territory but are independently authored; in many newcomer nuclear states and across the European Union, SSR-2/1 is incorporated more directly into licensing bases. The standard is complemented by SSR-2/2 (Rev. 1) on commissioning and operation and by a family of Specific Safety Guides (SSG series) that provide recommendations on how to meet individual SSR-2/1 requirements. SSR-2/1 (Rev. 1) remains the current edition as of 2026; the IAEA has not issued a Revision 2.

entries.short_definition: ITAAC are the inspections, tests, analyses, and acceptance criteria written into a 10 CFR Part 52 combined license that the NRC must find complete before fuel load.
entries.full_definition:
ITAAC stands for Inspections, Tests, Analyses, and Acceptance Criteria. They are the verification mechanism that bridges design certification and operation under 10 CFR Part 52, the one-step licensing framework the NRC issued in 1989 and that Congress reinforced through Title XXVIII of the Energy Policy Act of 1992. Because a combined license (COL) authorizes both construction and operation in a single proceeding, Congress and the Commission needed a deterministic way to confirm that the as-built plant matches the certified design before the licensee is allowed to load fuel. ITAAC are that mechanism, and they are codified principally in 10 CFR 52.97 (issuance of combined licenses) and 10 CFR 52.99 (inspection during construction; ITAAC schedules and notifications).

Under 52.97(b), the Commission must identify in each COL the inspections, tests, and analyses the licensee shall perform, together with the acceptance criteria that, if met, are necessary and sufficient to provide reasonable assurance that the facility has been constructed and will be operated in conformity with the license, the Atomic Energy Act, and the NRC's regulations. Each ITAAC therefore has a three-part structure: a design commitment drawn from the Tier 1 information of the referenced design certification (for example, Appendix D to Part 52 for the AP1000, finalized 27 January 2006 at 71 FR 4464), a method of verification (the inspection, test, or analysis), and an explicit acceptance criterion. Plant-specific ITAAC are listed in Appendix C of the COL.

Section 52.99 governs the closure workflow. The licensee submits an ITAAC Closure Notification (ICN) under 52.99(c)(1) explaining the basis for concluding that an ITAAC has been successfully completed, and notifies the NRC under 52.99(c)(4) once all ITAAC are complete. The NRC reviewer, working through the Inspection Manual and Regulatory Guide 1.215, performs construction inspection, evaluates each ICN, and publishes Federal Register notices of staff determinations. Only after the Commission makes the "all acceptance criteria are met" finding under 10 CFR 52.103(g) may operation commence; per 52.103(h), the ITAAC then cease to be regulatory requirements.

The AP1000 Vogtle units are the lived precedent. Southern Nuclear closed 398 ITAAC for Vogtle Unit 3 and the Commission issued the 52.103(g) finding on 3 August 2022, then 364 ITAAC for Vogtle Unit 4 with the finding on 28 July 2023, the first operational applications of the Part 52 ITAAC process in the United States.

entries.short_definition: Kairos Power's 35 MWt fluoride-salt-cooled high-temperature test reactor at Oak Ridge, the first non-light-water construction permit issued by the NRC in over 50 years.
entries.full_definition:
Kairos Hermes is the non-power demonstration test reactor authorized under NRC Docket No. 50-7513, a 35 megawatt-thermal fluoride-salt-cooled high-temperature reactor (KP-FHR) being built by Kairos Power, LLC at the Heritage Center Industrial Park in Oak Ridge, Tennessee. The design uses tri-structural isotropic (TRISO) particle fuel in pebble form cooled by molten fluoride salt (Flibe), and is not configured to generate electricity. The Hermes review was the first time NRC staff evaluated both TRISO fuel and a molten-salt coolant in support of a license or permit action (NRC, Hermes Kairos project page).

The construction permit (CPPR-1) was issued on 12 December 2023, making Hermes the first non-light-water reactor construction permit issued by the U.S. Nuclear Regulatory Commission in more than 50 years (Kairos Power; U.S. Department of Energy, Office of Nuclear Energy). The permit was granted under the two-step licensing framework of 10 CFR Part 50, with the operating license to be sought in a separate later application. The permit originally set 31 December 2026 as the latest date for completion of construction; the Commission extended that completion date by 28 months following a Kairos request (Federal Register, 18 May 2026).

Hermes is being executed under the Department of Energy's Advanced Reactor Demonstration Program, with collaboration from Oak Ridge National Laboratory and the Tennessee Valley Authority. Site preparation and groundbreaking occurred in July 2024, with excavation completed in October 2024. The project is the precursor to Hermes 2, a two-unit electricity-producing demonstration plant on the adjacent site whose construction permits were subsequently approved by the Commission.

For the U.S. advanced reactor community, Hermes is the canonical proof point that the existing Part 50 framework can license a Generation IV non-LWR on a credible schedule. It established licensing precedent for FHR safety analysis, source term, and mechanistic source-term methods later relevant to other non-LWR applicants pursuing 10 CFR Part 50, Part 52, and the forthcoming Part 53 pathways.

entries.short_definition: LIC-500 is the U.S. NRC Office of Nuclear Reactor Regulation office instruction governing the topical report process, most recently issued as Revision 9 in January 2022.
entries.full_definition:
LIC-500, "Topical Report Process," is the standing NRR office instruction that standardizes how the U.S. Nuclear Regulatory Commission staff accepts, reviews, and dispositions topical reports (TRs) submitted by vendors, owners groups, and licensees. Revision 9, issued January 27, 2022 under ADAMS accession number ML20247G279, replaced Revision 6 (2018) and aligned the process with parallel NRR instructions on acceptance reviews, requests for additional information (RAIs), and regulatory audits. A topical report under LIC-500 is a generic technical submittal addressing methodologies, codes, components, or analyses that an applicant intends to reference repeatedly in docketed actions in lieu of re-litigating the same content.

Revision 9 codifies four graded review pathways selected on the basis of complexity, novelty, and prior precedent. The Standard Review is the default pathway for highly complex or first-of-a-kind TRs and runs the full sequence of acceptance review, RAIs, draft safety evaluation (SE), final SE, and verification, on a schedule that is typically scoped to roughly two years. The Compressed Review compresses that same sequence on an agreed accelerated schedule for moderately complex submittals. The Uncomplicated Review applies to narrowly scoped TRs that build on established staff positions, and the SE Confirmation Review is reserved for TRs that essentially restate, with confirmatory analysis, conclusions already reached in a prior approved SE. For the Uncomplicated and Confirmation pathways, staff agreement to use the pathway itself constitutes acceptance and the formal acceptance review is abbreviated.

The terminal artifact of every pathway is the final SE, which is then bound with the as-reviewed report, the staff RAIs and applicant responses, and any conditions and limitations into the approved or "-A" version of the topical report. Per LIC-500, the NRC staff must verify the -A version before it may be cited as a regulatory basis. An applicant references an approved TR in a docketed application, such as a PSAR or FSAR submitted under 10 CFR Part 50 or Part 52, by listing the report number, revision, and -A designation, and by confirming compliance with each SE limitation and condition in the application text. Where the docketed action is a license amendment request, design certification, or combined license application, the referenced TR-SE is treated as part of the licensing basis and is subject to the additional legal review described in LIC-500 before issuance.

entries.short_definition: Nuclear Energy Institute guidance, Revision 1 (August 2019), defining the Licensing Modernization Project methodology for non-light-water reactor licensing basis development, endorsed by NRC Regulatory Guide 1.233.
entries.full_definition:
NEI 18-04, Revision 1, "Risk-Informed Performance-Based Technology Inclusive Guidance for Non-Light Water Reactor Licensing Basis Development," was issued by the Nuclear Energy Institute in August 2019 as the consensus output of the Licensing Modernization Project (LMP), a cost-shared effort led by industry and supported by the Department of Energy. The guidance establishes a technology-inclusive, risk-informed, and performance-based (TI-RIPB) methodology that any non-light-water reactor (non-LWR) applicant may use to develop the principal elements of its licensing basis, regardless of coolant, fuel form, or thermal spectrum. The NRC endorsed the document, with clarifications and points of emphasis but no significant exceptions, in Regulatory Guide 1.233, Revision 0, issued June 9, 2020, as an acceptable means of satisfying the relevant requirements of 10 CFR Parts 50 and 52.

The methodology rests on three integrated tasks. First, Licensing Basis Event (LBE) selection uses a probabilistic risk assessment that begins early in design to identify the full spectrum of event sequences and bin them by mean frequency into Anticipated Operational Occurrences (AOOs), Design Basis Events (DBEs), and Beyond Design Basis Events (BDBEs), with a separate deterministic set of Design Basis Accidents (DBAs) derived from the DBEs. Each LBE is then plotted against the Frequency-Consequence (F-C) Target curve in Figure 3-1 of NEI 18-04, which sets dose limits that decrease as event frequency increases, providing a single integrated acceptance criterion across the event space.

Second, structures, systems, and components (SSCs) are classified by their safety function in the selected LBEs. SSCs required to perform a Required Safety Function for any DBA are designated Safety-Related (SR); SSCs that are risk-significant or that support defense-in-depth adequacy but are not credited in DBAs are designated Non-Safety-Related with Special Treatment (NSRST); the remainder are Non-Safety-Related with No Special Treatment (NST). Special treatments are graded to risk significance rather than imposed uniformly.

Third, defense-in-depth (DID) adequacy is assessed through an integrated evaluation, drawing on the layer-specific and plant-capability guidelines in Section 3.3 of NEI 18-04 and the cumulative risk targets benchmarked against the NRC's Safety Goal Policy Statement. The framework is now the principal pathway under 10 CFR Part 53 and underlies recent advanced-reactor applications including Kairos Hermes and the Oklo Aurora effort, and informs the technology-inclusive content guidance later issued as NEI 21-07 and Draft Regulatory Guide DG-1404.

entries.short_definition: NEI 21-07 Revision 1 is the Technology Inclusive Content of Application Project guideline, endorsed by NRC Regulatory Guide 1.253 in April 2024, defining a risk-informed, performance-based safety analysis report format for non-LWR license applications.
entries.full_definition:
NEI 21-07, Revision 1, "Technology Inclusive Guidance for Non-Light Water Reactors, Safety Analysis Report Content: For Applicants Using the NEI 18-04 Methodology" (issued February 2022), is the industry-developed deliverable of the Technology Inclusive Content of Application Project (TICAP). It prescribes the scope and depth of information an applicant must provide in the safety case portions of a non-LWR safety analysis report when the licensing basis is developed under the Licensing Modernization Project methodology of NEI 18-04 (endorsed by RG 1.233). NEI 21-07 systematically tracks the three LMP pillars: selection of licensing basis events, safety classification and special treatment of structures, systems, and components, and assessment of defense-in-depth adequacy.

The Nuclear Regulatory Commission endorsed NEI 21-07, Revision 1, by issuing Regulatory Guide 1.253, Revision 0, with the Federal Register notice of availability published April 3, 2024. RG 1.253 accepts the methodology as one acceptable means of preparing safety analysis report content for a construction permit or operating license under 10 CFR Part 50, or a combined license or design certification under 10 CFR Part 52, while adding NRC clarifications and additions on points such as functional containment, the treatment of required functional design criteria and complementary design criteria, and SSC categorization into Safety-Related and Non-Safety-Related with Special Treatment.

Structurally, NEI 21-07 covers the first eight chapters of the SAR and is deliberately modular rather than tied to the NUREG-0800 standard review plan outline used for LWRs. Chapter 1 frames the design and safety case, Chapter 2 develops the LBEs, Chapters 3 through 7 organize SSC classification, design criteria, and integrated performance against LBEs, and Chapter 8 consolidates cross-cutting analyses and analytical tools that support multiple LBEs or SSCs, so the safety case can be evaluated from the content of Chapters 2 through 8.

TICAP sits alongside, not under, RG 1.206. RG 1.206 (revised October 2018) defines a 19-chapter LWR-oriented application format; the TICAP/ARCAP combination produces a roughly 12-chapter non-LWR application in which NEI 21-07 governs the LMP-derived safety case chapters and the parallel Advanced Reactor Content of Application Project (ARCAP, RG 1.254) governs the remaining programmatic and operational chapters. Applicants such as Kairos, X-energy, and TerraPower have aligned advanced reactor submittals to this framework.

entries.short_definition: NEIMA is the Nuclear Energy Innovation and Modernization Act, Public Law 115-439 (January 14, 2019), directing the NRC to build a technology-inclusive regulatory framework and reform fee recovery for advanced reactors.
entries.full_definition:
The Nuclear Energy Innovation and Modernization Act (NEIMA), Public Law 115-439, was signed on January 14, 2019. It restructured how the U.S. Nuclear Regulatory Commission funds itself and licenses new commercial reactors, with the explicit aim of preparing the agency to review non-light-water and other advanced reactor designs. NEIMA frames "advanced nuclear reactor" broadly and defines a "technology-inclusive regulatory framework" as one developed using methods of evaluation that are flexible and practicable for application to a variety of reactor technologies, including risk-informed and performance-based techniques where appropriate.

Section 102 overhauled NRC fee recovery. It capped annual fees for operating power reactors at the FY 2015 per-licensee level adjusted for inflation, and it carved out "excluded activities" that the NRC may not bill to licensees, including advanced reactor regulatory infrastructure, generic homeland security work, university research, and a reduced hourly rate for advanced reactor applicants and pre-applicants. Section 102 also authorized fee relief for early site permit reviews on Department of Energy or critical national security infrastructure sites, the statutory hook later used for activities such as the Idaho National Laboratory siting work for advanced demonstrations.

Section 103 is the licensing mandate. Section 103(a)(4) directed the NRC to complete a rulemaking establishing a technology-inclusive regulatory framework for optional use by commercial advanced reactor applicants no later than December 31, 2027. That rulemaking became 10 CFR Part 53, "Risk-Informed, Technology-Inclusive Regulatory Framework for Commercial Nuclear Plants," which the Commission published as a final rule in the Federal Register on March 30, 2026 with an effective date of April 29, 2026, more than a year ahead of the statutory deadline. Part 53 sits alongside, not in place of, the existing 10 CFR Part 50 and 10 CFR Part 52 pathways.

To bridge the gap before Part 53 took effect, NEIMA also drove the Advanced Reactor Content of Application Project (ARCAP) and the related industry Technology-Inclusive Content of Application Project (TI-CAP, NEI 21-07), which translate the statute's technology-inclusive intent into application content guidance usable under Parts 50 and 52. NEIMA further required staged reports to Congress on advanced reactor licensing, expedited environmental reviews, and the establishment of staff expertise and computer codes needed to evaluate non-light-water designs, making it the primary statutory basis for the NRC's current advanced reactor licensing modernization program.

entries.short_definition: DANU is the NRC Office of Nuclear Reactor Regulation division that since 2022 has led pre-application engagement and licensing review for advanced reactors and non-power facilities.
entries.full_definition:
The Division of Advanced Reactors and Non-Power Production and Utilization Facilities, known by its NRC organizational code DANU, sits inside the Office of Nuclear Reactor Regulation (NRR) and is the focal point for staff review of non-light-water reactors, small modular reactors, and research and test reactors. It was stood up in 2022 as part of the post-merger restructuring of NRR, which had absorbed the legacy Office of New Reactors (NRO) on October 13, 2019. The 2019 merger was driven by a depressed new-build pipeline and a Commission directive to concentrate advanced-reactor expertise alongside operating-fleet regulators; DANU is the division that materialized inside NRR to carry the advanced-reactor portfolio forward.

DANU is led by director Mohamed Shams, who moved from Deputy Director of the Division of Operating Reactor Licensing into the role. The division is organized around an Advanced Reactor Policy Branch and project-management branches (the Advanced Reactor Licensing Branches, including UTB1 and UTB2 in the NRC's internal taxonomy) plus the Non-Power Production and Utilization Facilities Licensing Branch. Its scope spans both reactor and non-reactor work: pre-application engagement, construction permit and operating license review under 10 CFR Part 50, combined license review under 10 CFR Part 52, the technology-inclusive framework being built out under 10 CFR Part 53, and oversight of the nation's research reactors, test reactors, and medical-isotope production facilities.

DANU staff own the dockets for the highest-profile advanced reactor reviews of the decade. These include Kairos Power's Hermes test reactor (Docket 05007513) and Hermes 2 (Dockets 05000611/0500612), the Kairos pre-application file (Docket 99902069), TerraPower's Natrium construction permit for Kemmerer Power Station Unit 1 (docketed in May 2024, with the safety evaluation completed in December 2025), and pre-application engagements with X-energy, Holtec's SMR-300, and others. The division also issues the DANU-ISG series of Interim Staff Guidance documents that operationalize the Advanced Reactor Content of Application Project (ARCAP) and the Technology-Inclusive Content of Application Project (TICAP), giving applicants a chapter-by-chapter map of expected PSAR/FSAR content.

DANU's run as the lead advanced-reactor division is finite. Under the NRC's 2026 reorganization, a new Office of Advanced Reactors (OAR) is being stood up by mid-summer 2026 to consolidate advanced-reactor licensing in its own office, absorbing much of DANU's portfolio. Until the OAR transition is complete, DANU remains the docketed reviewing division of record for the active advanced-reactor applications cited above.

entries.short_definition: NuScale's small modular reactor plant family, built from 77 MWe NuScale Power Modules; the original 50 MWe variant was certified by the NRC in 2023 under 10 CFR Part 52 Appendix G.
entries.full_definition:
VOYGR is NuScale Power's branded plant offering built from integral pressurized-water NuScale Power Modules (NPMs), each housed in its own containment vessel and sharing a common safety-related pool. The plant scales by module count: VOYGR-4, VOYGR-6, and VOYGR-12 configurations have been marketed, with VOYGR-6 (six modules, 462 MWe net) positioned as NuScale's principal commercial offering on the uprated 77 MWe module.

On 19 January 2023 the U.S. Nuclear Regulatory Commission published the final rule certifying the original NuScale standard design, codified as Appendix G to 10 CFR Part 52 with a 15-year applicability period and effective 21 February 2023 (Federal Register, 88 FR 3287; Docket No. 52-048). That certified design rates each NPM at 50 MWe with a maximum plant configuration of 12 modules (600 MWe). It is the first SMR design and the seventh light-water reactor design certified by the NRC. A combined license applicant under 10 CFR 52 Subpart C may reference Appendix G and inherit the resolved technical and safety issues, subject to the Tier 1, Tier 2, and operational programs frozen in the rule.

The uprated VOYGR-6 plant is licensed through a separate path. NuScale submitted a dual application to the NRC on 1 January 2023 covering the NuScale US460 standard design: a Standard Design Approval (SDA) under 10 CFR Part 52 Subpart E and a Standard Design Certification (SDC) application under Subpart B (Docket Nos. 052-00050 and 099902078). The SDA was issued on 29 May 2025, raising the per-module rating from 50 to 77 MWe and reducing the maximum module count from 12 to 6. The companion SDC rulemaking, which would amend Part 52 to add a new appendix for the US460 design, remained under NRC review at the time of the SDA issuance.

NuScale VOYGR should not be confused with Holtec's SMR-300 (formerly SMR-160), an unrelated 300 MWe integral PWR pursued under a separate construction-permit pathway. Within nuclear practice, VOYGR is commonly referenced alongside the broader Part 52 architecture (combined licenses, early site permits, ITAAC) and the LWR-specific guidance that governed NuScale's review, including the Standard Review Plan and Regulatory Guide 1.206 on combined-license applications.

entries.short_definition: A 1.5 MWe sodium-cooled fast microreactor whose custom combined license application became the first advanced-reactor COLA denied without prejudice by the NRC on January 6, 2022.
entries.full_definition:
Oklo Aurora is a compact, sodium-cooled fast microreactor designed by Oklo Inc. for a nominal 1.5 MWe (roughly 4 MWth) powerhouse fueled by recycled high-assay low-enriched uranium (HALEU) and using supercritical CO2 power conversion. The proposed first-of-a-kind plant, sited on the Idaho National Laboratory (INL) campus, became the inaugural test case for the NRC's combined license (COL) pathway under 10 CFR Part 52 applied to a non-light-water microreactor.

Oklo Power LLC submitted a custom COLA on March 11, 2020, which the NRC docketed on June 5, 2020 (Docket No. 52-049). Because the Aurora design preceded the Part 53 risk-informed framework, Oklo pursued a bespoke Part 52 submission that combined site, design, and operating authorization in a single docket and proposed alternative methodologies for accident selection in lieu of a conventional probabilistic risk assessment (PRA). Over the next twenty-one months the staff issued three rounds of Requests for Additional Information (RAIs), audited topical reports, and held public meetings. Oklo supplemented the docket in July and October 2021, but the staff found that the response set still did not close out fundamental gaps.

On January 6, 2022, the NRC denied the application without prejudice in letter ML21357A034 and announced the action through press release 22-002. The denial cited "significant information gaps" in three areas: (1) the maximum credible accident (MCA) and accident-initiator selection methodology, which the staff found lacked a rigorous, repeatable substitute for a PRA; (2) the classification of safety-related structures, systems, and components and their associated performance and reliability requirements; and (3) the maturity of the quality assurance program applied to design and fabrication. Without prejudice meant Oklo retained the right to resubmit, but the staff concluded it could neither set a review schedule nor reach the safety findings required by 10 CFR 52.97 on the existing record.

The denial is a recurring risk factor in Oklo's 2024 Form S-1, which acknowledges that no Aurora powerhouse has been licensed by the NRC or any other regulator and that resubmission timing and outcome remain uncertain. Oklo has since restarted pre-application engagement and signaled a phased relicensing approach informed by NEI 21-07 (TICAP) and the advanced reactor content of application project (ARCAP), positioning a future Aurora submittal closer to the licensing modernization regime now codified for non-LWR designs.

entries.short_definition: The PSAR and FSAR are the two-stage Safety Analysis Reports an applicant submits to the U.S. NRC under 10 CFR 50.34 to obtain a construction permit and operating license.
entries.full_definition:
The Preliminary Safety Analysis Report (PSAR) and Final Safety Analysis Report (FSAR) are the principal technical submissions in the two-step reactor licensing process codified at 10 CFR 50.34. Under 10 CFR 50.34(a), a PSAR accompanies an application for a construction permit (CP) and presents preliminary design information, site characteristics, and a discussion of postulated accidents and proposed safety features sufficient for the NRC staff to conclude that the facility can be built and operated without undue risk. Under 10 CFR 50.34(b), the FSAR accompanies the operating license (OL) application and documents the as-built, final design, operating procedures, technical specifications, and supporting analyses. In the Part 52 combined license (COL) pathway, a single FSAR is filed up front, incorporating the certified design.

Both reports follow the standard format established by NRC Regulatory Guide 1.70, Revision 3, "Standard Format and Content of Safety Analysis Reports for Nuclear Power Plants, LWR Edition," issued November 1978 by the Office of Standards Development. RG 1.70 organizes the SAR into nineteen chapters: (1) Introduction and General Description of Plant; (2) Site Characteristics; (3) Design of Structures, Components, Equipment, and Systems; (4) Reactor; (5) Reactor Coolant System and Connected Systems; (6) Engineered Safety Features; (7) Instrumentation and Controls; (8) Electric Power; (9) Auxiliary Systems; (10) Steam and Power Conversion System; (11) Radioactive Waste Management; (12) Radiation Protection; (13) Conduct of Operations; (14) Initial Test Program; (15) Accident Analyses (transient and design-basis); (16) Technical Specifications; (17) Quality Assurance; (18) Human Factors Engineering; and (19) Probabilistic Risk Assessment and Severe Accident Evaluation. Chapters 18 and 19 were added with the new-reactor regulatory framework; Chapter 19 became formal SRP content in the 2007 Revision of NUREG-0800.

NRC staff evaluate each chapter against the corresponding section of NUREG-0800, the Standard Review Plan (originally NUREG-75/087, renumbered in 1980), whose section numbering tracks RG 1.70 one-for-one. Modern FSARs are large multivolume documents; industry practice typically runs to several thousand pages with extensive technical appendices, although the NRC sets no fixed length. The SAR is a living licensing-basis document: once issued, the FSAR is maintained as the Updated FSAR (UFSAR) under 10 CFR 50.71(e), with revisions submitted at least every 24 months. Both RG 1.70 and NUREG-0800 remain anchored to large light-water reactor designs; the NRC's Part 53 framework and forthcoming guidance are intended to provide a more technology-inclusive content standard for non-LWRs.

entries.short_definition: A Request for Additional Information is the formal NRC docket mechanism by which staff ask a license applicant to supply or clarify information needed to complete a regulatory finding.
entries.full_definition:
A Request for Additional Information, or RAI, is the principal written instrument through which U.S. Nuclear Regulatory Commission staff obtain information from a license applicant or licensee that is needed to support a regulatory finding under 10 CFR Part 50, Part 52, or Part 53. Each RAI is logged into the docket as a numbered question tied to a specific Standard Review Plan (NUREG-0800) section and to the part of the application, such as a chapter of the Preliminary Safety Analysis Report or Final Safety Analysis Report, that staff cannot accept without further clarification. RAI processing is governed by NRR Office Instruction LIC-115, "Processing Requests for Additional Information," Revision 1, issued August 5, 2021, which directs staff to limit RAIs to information that is required to make a regulatory determination, to consider audits as an alternative to written RAIs, and to coordinate draft RAIs with the applicant before formal issuance (NRC LIC-115).

In practice an RAI follows a defined cycle. Staff issue a draft RAI, the applicant has roughly one week for clarification calls, and a final RAI letter is then docketed in ADAMS. LIC-115 establishes a default response window of 30 days from issuance of the final RAI, though extensions are routinely negotiated for complex technical questions, and the project schedule is adjusted accordingly. Each large-light-water-reactor combined license application page on nrc.gov, such as the Callaway Unit 2 and Levy County RAI pages, lists the RAI letters by Standard Review Plan section and links to the applicant's responses, illustrating how the docket is structured around RAIs as discrete review artifacts (NRC Callaway RAI page).

Volumes per application are large. Public RAI portals for individual COL and design certification dockets routinely contain several hundred RAI letters, each often bundling dozens of individual questions, so total question counts can run into the low thousands across an AP1000 COL or a design certification such as the NuScale US600. NRC does not publish a single aggregate count, and GAO reported in GAO-17-344 (May 2017) that the agency historically "does not specifically track the number of RAIs" across offices, which itself contributed to inconsistent oversight of the process (GAO-17-344).

Because each RAI cycle consumes review hours and applicant engineering effort, RAI volume is the dominant driver of schedule slippage in NRC reviews. The NRC Inspector General's 2015 report and subsequent GAO review flagged the time and resources required as a central concern, motivating LIC-115 and ongoing reform efforts to reduce multiple RAI rounds and to focus questions narrowly on safety findings.

entries.short_definition: Regulatory Guide 1.206 Revision 1 (October 2018) is the NRC's consolidated guidance on the format and content of 10 CFR Part 52 applications for design certifications, early site permits, and combined licenses.
entries.full_definition:
RG 1.206, "Applications for Nuclear Power Plants," establishes the NRC staff's expected structure and depth for applications submitted under 10 CFR Part 52. Revision 1, issued October 12, 2018, supersedes the June 2007 Revision 0 (which had been titled "Combined License Applications for Nuclear Power Plants (LWR Edition)") and broadens the scope from combined licenses (COLs) alone to also cover standard design certifications (DCs), early site permits (ESPs), and limited work authorizations. For new light-water reactors, RG 1.206 functionally replaces RG 1.70, "Standard Format and Content of Safety Analysis Reports for Nuclear Power Plants (LWR Edition)," which had governed Part 50 construction permit and operating license SARs since the 1970s. RG 1.70 remains the format reference for the legacy Part 50 fleet, while RG 1.206 is the controlling format guide for new Part 52 reactors.

The guide is organized in two regulatory positions. Section C.1 prescribes the standard format and content of the application itself, including the Final Safety Analysis Report chapter structure (Chapters 1 through 19, mirroring NUREG-0800), the environmental report, emergency plans, and ITAAC. Section C.2 covers regulatory process topics for preparation, submittal, acceptance review, and docketing. For a COL applicant that references a certified design, RG 1.206 carries forward the four-category C-I-S-A acceptance pattern for partitioning FSAR information: Conceptual design information from the DCD that the COL applicant must complete; information Identical to the certified DCD that is incorporated by reference; Site-specific information unique to the proposed location; and Alternate information where the applicant departs from the certified design, which must be identified and justified. This taxonomy lets the staff focus its review on what is genuinely new while accepting the certified scope as resolved.

RG 1.206 is tightly aligned with NUREG-0800, the Standard Review Plan. The 2018 revision deliberately removed detailed technical content from Revision 0 that had become redundant with the SRP, leaving RG 1.206 as the format and content roadmap and the SRP as the technical acceptance criteria. The guide cross-references each FSAR chapter to the corresponding SRP section, and the NRC withdrew four COL/DC/ESP interim staff guidance documents (COL-ISG-024, -026, -027, and -028) on issuance because their content was folded into Revision 1. Applicants for SMRs and non-LWR designs may also use RG 1.206 as a starting point, with departures documented per the C-I-S-A framework, though Part 53 applicants will eventually rely on technology-inclusive guidance such as NEI 21-07 (TICAP) and ARCAP.

entries.short_definition: NRC Regulatory Guide 1.215 describes a method the staff finds acceptable for documenting closure of inspections, tests, analyses, and acceptance criteria under 10 CFR Part 52.
entries.full_definition:
Regulatory Guide 1.215, "Guidance for ITAAC Closure Under 10 CFR Part 52," is issued by the U.S. Nuclear Regulatory Commission to describe a method the staff considers acceptable for satisfying the licensee notification and documentation requirements in 10 CFR 52.99. The current revision is Revision 2, issued in July 2015 after publication of Draft Regulatory Guide DG-1316 for public comment in January 2015. It is filed in ADAMS under Accession No. ML15105A447. Revision 2 supersedes Revision 1 (September 2011, ML112580018) and the original guide (June 2009, ML091480076).

The guide endorses Nuclear Energy Institute (NEI) 08-01, "Industry Guideline for the ITAAC Closure Process Under 10 CFR Part 52," Revision 5 - Corrected (ML14182A160), subject to exceptions and additional staff guidance stated in the regulatory positions. NEI 08-01 supplies the format, content, and process expectations that combined license holders use when preparing ITAAC closure notifications (ICNs) under 10 CFR 52.99(c)(1), uncompleted ITAAC notifications (UICNs) under 10 CFR 52.99(c)(2), and the post-completion notification under 10 CFR 52.99(c)(4) that supports the Commission finding under 10 CFR 52.103(g) before fuel load.

Revision 2 expands the prior guide in three principal directions. First, it incorporates ITAAC maintenance expectations for design acceptance criteria (DAC) and the design reliability assurance program (D-RAP), addressing how licensees track and document changes to as-built information after an ITAAC determination of completion. Second, it adopts lessons learned from the simulated ITAAC closure pilots conducted with Vogtle Units 3 and 4 and V.C. Summer Units 2 and 3, including refinements to the information and formatting expected in UICNs so that the staff has sufficient information to make timely findings. Third, it clarifies treatment of post-ICN changes and the relationship between ITAAC closure submittals and the Final Safety Analysis Report.

The guide applies to applicants and holders of combined licenses, early site permits with ITAAC, and standard design approvals or certifications referenced in a Part 52 license. It is read together with Regulatory Guide 1.206 on COL applications and the NRC inspection program implementing 10 CFR Part 52, Subpart H. Although nominally a method document, in practice it functions as the controlling format and content reference for every ITAAC submittal made under Part 52 today.

entries.short_definition: NRC guidance, issued June 2020 as Revision 0, that endorses NEI 18-04 as one acceptable technology-inclusive, risk-informed, performance-based methodology for non-light-water reactor licensing.
entries.full_definition:
Regulatory Guide 1.233, "Guidance for a Technology-Inclusive, Risk-Informed, and Performance-Based Methodology to Inform the Licensing Basis and Content of Applications for Licenses, Certifications, and Approvals for Non-Light-Water Reactors," was issued by the U.S. Nuclear Regulatory Commission as Revision 0 and announced in the Federal Register on June 9, 2020. The guide endorses, with clarifications, the methodology in NEI 18-04, Revision 1, "Risk-Informed Performance-Based Technology-Inclusive Guidance for Non-Light Water Reactor Licensing Basis Development," as one acceptable means to inform the licensing basis and the scope and level of detail of applications submitted under 10 CFR Part 50 and 10 CFR Part 52.

The endorsed methodology, developed through the industry-led Licensing Modernization Project, is built around three integrated tasks: identifying licensing basis events (LBEs) using probabilistic risk assessment, classifying structures, systems, and components (SSCs) by safety function, and evaluating the adequacy of defense in depth. LBEs are binned as anticipated operational occurrences, design basis events, design basis accidents, and beyond design basis events, and are screened against a frequency-consequence target that anchors the risk-informed evaluation. SSCs are assigned to one of three categories, namely safety-related, non-safety-related with special treatment, and non-safety-related with no special treatment, with the higher categories carrying corresponding design code, quality assurance, and special treatment expectations. RG 1.233 states that applicants using the methodology are expected to follow NEI 18-04 terminology and to identify any needed exemptions from existing regulations.

RG 1.233 is technology-inclusive and therefore applies to gas-cooled, sodium-cooled, molten-salt, and other non-LWR designs, including small modular and microreactor concepts. Several early advanced-reactor licensing efforts, including the Kairos Hermes construction permit and the TerraPower Natrium and X-energy Xe-100 preapplication interactions, have used the RG 1.233 framework. The guide is the foundational document for the subsequent NRC content-of-application guidance issued as Regulatory Guide 1.253, which endorses NEI 21-07 (TICAP) and complements the agency's broader Advanced Reactor Content of Application Project (ARCAP). RG 1.233 does not itself create new regulatory requirements; it identifies a method the staff considers acceptable and continues to coexist with traditional deterministic licensing approaches under 10 CFR Part 50 and Part 52, and with the technology-inclusive framework being developed under 10 CFR Part 53.

entries.short_definition: The Commercial Space Transportation Advisory Committee is a FACA-chartered federal advisory body that advises the Secretary of Transportation, through the FAA, on commercial space transportation policy and regulation.
entries.full_definition:
COMSTAC was established in 1984 alongside the Commercial Space Launch Act and operates under the Federal Advisory Committee Act (Public Law 92-463). It provides information, advice, and recommendations to the Secretary of Transportation, delivered through the FAA Office of Commercial Space Transportation (AST), on the U.S. commercial space transportation industry. Membership is capped at roughly 20 senior representatives from launch and reentry operators, satellite and insurance firms, legal and financial advisors, state aerospace authorities, and academia, appointed by the Secretary for up to two consecutive two-year terms (FAA).

The committee organizes its work through standing working groups, including the Regulatory Working Group, the Safety Working Group, and the Operations Working Group, which prepare taskers and reports adopted at semiannual public plenaries noticed in the Federal Register. Recent attention has centered on 14 CFR Part 450, the consolidated launch and reentry licensing rule that took effect on March 10, 2021 and to which all legacy Part 415, 417, and 431 licenses must migrate by March 10, 2026. In a July 2023 report, the Regulatory Working Group documented systemic implementation problems with Part 450, including thin FAA technical capacity to evaluate novel means of compliance (MoCs), inconsistent application of performance-based requirements, and unpredictable pre-application timelines (FAA).

COMSTAC findings have shaped subsequent federal action. The committee's Part 450 critique fed directly into the FAA's October 2024 chartering of the Aerospace Rulemaking Committee (SpARC), tasked with delivering recommended revisions to nine Part 450 topic areas, among them flight safety analysis, system safety, and MoC governance, by late summer 2025. The committee's recommendations on MoC acceptance, pre-application consultation, and high-cadence operations were also referenced in the August 13, 2025 White House Executive Order directing the FAA to reevaluate, amend, or rescind Part 450 requirements (Congressional Research Service R48582).

Beyond launch licensing, COMSTAC tracks spaceport infrastructure, human spaceflight occupant safety, space traffic coordination with NOAA and the FCC, and U.S. positions before international bodies such as the ITU and the UN Committee on the Peaceful Uses of Outer Space. Reports and meeting materials are public and posted by AST, but COMSTAC itself holds no rulemaking authority; its outputs are advisory inputs that the FAA may incorporate into notices of proposed rulemaking, advisory circulars such as AC 450.41-1, or internal licensing guidance.
