entries.short_definition: ECSS is the European Cooperation for Space Standardization, a coherent single set of standards for space project management, engineering, and product assurance maintained by ESA, industry, and national agencies.
entries.full_definition:
The European Cooperation for Space Standardization (ECSS) is the unified European framework of standards used across space project management, engineering, and product assurance. It was established in 1993 at the initiative of Eurospace and formally adopted by the European Space Agency on 23 June 1994 under resolution ESA/C/CXIII/Res.1, replacing the earlier PSS (Procedures, Specifications and Standards) series. Full members include ESA, Eurospace, and the national space agencies of France (CNES), Germany (DLR), Italy (ASI), the Netherlands (NSO), Norway (NOSA), and the United Kingdom (UKSA), with the Canadian Space Agency as an associate. The Secretariat is hosted at ESA-ESTEC in Noordwijk and the document set is published through ecss.nl.

The system is organized into branches identified by a single letter in the document code. The four user-facing standardization disciplines are the M-branch (Management — planning, cost, schedule, risk, configuration), the E-branch (Engineering — system, mechanical, electrical, software, communications, control), the Q-branch (Quality and product assurance — including parts, materials and processes, dependability, and safety), and the more recent U-branch covering space sustainability topics such as debris mitigation and planetary protection. Three further branches (P, S, D) define the ECSS system itself, and a new I-branch covering industrialization, production and maintenance has been added. Document codes follow the form ECSS-Branch-Type-Number-Version, for example ECSS-E-ST-10-03C Rev.1, where "ST" denotes a Standard. According to ecss.nl, the active set comprises on the order of 139 documents.

Within the engineering branch, the E-10 family covers system engineering fundamentals. ECSS-E-ST-10C Rev.1 (15 February 2017) gives the general system-engineering requirements, ECSS-E-ST-10-02C Rev.1 (1 February 2018) covers verification, and ECSS-E-ST-10-03C Rev.1 (31 May 2022) is the testing standard. The testing standard defines the requirements for ground verification by test of space-segment elements and equipment, distinguishing qualification, acceptance, and protoflight approaches and specifying environmental test sequences, margins, durations, retest and redundancy testing. It is the European counterpart in scope to test-program documents such as NASA-GEVS, though structured around the ECSS qualification-model philosophy.

ECSS standards are not law. They become binding only when called up in a contract. In practice ESA invokes the relevant ECSS documents in its procurement Statements of Work and General Conditions of Tender, and prime contractors flow them down to suppliers with project-specific tailoring. National agencies and many commercial European primes adopt the same mechanism, which is why ECSS conformance, together with the resulting verification matrix against the cited E, M, and Q standards, is typically the spine of an ESA-procured mission's quality and engineering documentation.

entries.short_definition: ECSS-E-ST-10-03C Rev.1 (31 May 2022) is the European space engineering standard defining ground verification testing requirements for spacecraft segment elements and equipment before launch.
entries.full_definition:
ECSS-E-ST-10-03C Rev.1, issued by the ECSS Secretariat on 31 May 2022, is the European Cooperation for Space Standardization branch standard governing verification by testing of space segment elements and equipment on the ground prior to launch. It cancels and replaces the prior ECSS-E-ST-10-03C edition dated 1 June 2012, and was adopted by CEN as EN 16603-10-03:2022. The standard is published openly through ecss.nl and forms the testing pillar of the wider ECSS-E-10 engineering branch, working alongside ECSS-E-ST-10-02C on verification.

The scope covers test programme management, environmental testing, functional and performance testing, retest and redundancy testing, test margins and factors, measurement uncertainties, and development testing across qualification, acceptance, and protoflight models. It addresses the full range of environmental disciplines applied to spacecraft and payloads, including mechanical vibration, acoustic, mechanical shock, thermal vacuum, thermal at room and mission pressure, electromagnetic compatibility, and other environmental exposures, while pointing to discipline-specific ECSS standards for detailed methods. The standard explicitly excludes space system level testing, in-orbit testing, software verification, two-phase heat transport equipment, launcher segment testing, and ground segment testing.

The document is structured around normative annexes that define the deliverable requirements: Annex A specifies the Assembly, Integration and Test Plan, Annex B the Test Specification or TSPE in eleven sections covering approach, requirements, facility, sequence, and pass or fail criteria, and Annex C the Test Procedure or TPRO with step-by-step conduct instructions. Annex D, informative, gives tailoring and verification guidance. Notable changes from the 2012 edition include clarified scope wording and updated thermal test definitions distinguishing thermal vacuum test, thermal test at room pressure, and thermal test at mission pressure.

The companion handbook ECSS-E-HB-10-03A, also dated 31 May 2022, provides non-normative guidance keyed specifically to Revision 1 and references ECSS-E-HB-32-25 for mechanical shock design and ECSS-E-HB-31-01 for thermal design. Together the standard and handbook are the primary contractual reference invoked by ESA and European primes for spacecraft qualification and acceptance campaigns, and they govern the test evidence flowing into the verification control documents produced under ECSS-E-ST-10-02.

entries.short_definition: FAA Advisory Circular 450.41-1 describes the process for submitting and obtaining acceptance of a means of compliance under 14 CFR 450.35 for commercial launch and reentry licensing.
entries.full_definition:
FAA Advisory Circular 450.41-1 is the Office of Commercial Space Transportation (AST) guidance that describes the means of compliance (MoC) process established by 14 CFR 450.35. It explains how a prospective launch or reentry operator licensed under 14 CFR Part 450 may propose, document, and obtain Administrator acceptance of an alternative method for satisfying a performance-based safety requirement, either inside a license application or in advance of one. The AC implements the streamlined launch and reentry rule published December 10, 2020, which took effect March 10, 2021, and is structured around the language of 450.35(a) and (b).

Five Part 450 requirements demand a pre-accepted means of compliance before the FAA will accept a license application as complete: flight safety analyses under 450.115(b)(1); toxic hazards for flight under 450.139(e)(1); a highly reliable flight safety system under 450.145(b); lightning hazard mitigation under 450.163(a)(1); and toxic hazards mitigation for ground operations under 450.187(e)(1). For all other Subpart C performance requirements, an applicant may either invoke an MoC the FAA has already accepted, such as a published Part 450 Advisory Circular listed on the Part 450 Means of Compliance Table, or submit a tailored MoC for case-by-case review. The AC clarifies that an AC presents one acceptable, not exclusive, means of compliance, and other approaches may be approved under 450.35(a)(1).

The AC describes the expected content of an MoC document: identification of the regulatory text being satisfied, a description of the proposed methodology with assumptions and inputs, defined entrance and exit criteria, the data products and verification artifacts that demonstrate the criteria are met, and a configuration-control approach so the FAA can determine whether later changes affect the basis of acceptance. Submittals are made in a form and manner acceptable to the Administrator, and the AC points applicants to the pre-application consultation mechanisms under Part 413 and the complete-application guidance in AC 413.13-1.

FAA review is performed by AST technical staff and culminates in either an acceptance letter, a request for additional information, or a non-acceptance with rationale. An accepted MoC binds only the applicant that submitted it and the configuration described in the submittal; the FAA may revisit acceptance if the underlying safety analysis changes. The AC frames MoC acceptance as decoupled from, and a prerequisite to, the policy review under 450.41, the payload determination under 450.43, and the safety review under 450.47.

entries.short_definition: FAA Part 450 is the performance-based 14 CFR rule that consolidates commercial launch and reentry licensing into a single, vehicle-agnostic framework, effective March 10, 2021.
entries.full_definition:
FAA Part 450, codified at 14 CFR Part 450 and titled "Launch and Reentry License Requirements," is the U.S. commercial space transportation licensing regime administered by the FAA Office of Commercial Space Transportation (AST). The final rule was published in the Federal Register on December 10, 2020, and took effect on March 10, 2021, replacing four legacy prescriptive parts: Part 415 and Part 417 (expendable launch vehicles), Part 431 (reusable launch vehicles), and Part 435 (reentry vehicles). Per the transition provisions, operators with active licenses or applications accepted before June 8, 2021 may continue under the legacy parts until March 10, 2026, after which Parts 415, 417, 431, and 435 are removed (Federal Register 85 FR 79566; eCFR Title 14 Part 450).

The rule's central shift is from vehicle-specific prescriptive design rules to performance-based safety criteria backed by an operator-developed safety case. Subpart C ("Safety Requirements") sets the quantitative ceilings: collective public risk no greater than 1 x 10^-4 expected casualties (Ec) per operation, neighboring operations personnel risk no greater than 2 x 10^-4 Ec, and protection against high-consequence events in uncontrolled areas not to exceed 1 x 10^-3 conditional expected casualties unless demonstrated reliability is accepted by the Administrator (Section 450.101). Flight safety analysis scope and methods are set in Sections 450.113 and 450.115, with flight abort, flight safety systems, and trajectory and debris analyses defined as performance objectives rather than fixed hardware lists. Subpart B governs the application itself, including the policy and payload reviews and the environmental review under NEPA (eCFR Title 14 Part 450).

Compliance is established through a Means of Compliance (MoC). For most requirements an applicant may propose its own MoC, but Section 450.35 lists five areas, including flight safety analysis methods, high-reliability flight safety systems, lightning commit criteria, and toxic exposure thresholds for flight and ground, where the MoC must be pre-accepted by the FAA before an application can be submitted. The FAA publishes the running "Part 450 Means of Compliance Table" listing accepted methods and advisory circulars (FAA.gov Streamlined Licensing Process).

Procedurally, an applicant enters pre-application consultation, submits a complete application, and the statutory 180-day clock under 51 U.S.C. 50905 begins only when the FAA deems the application "sufficiently complete." During review the FAA issues written questions, commonly referred to in industry as Requests for Additional Information (RAIs), and the clock is paused while the applicant responds; iterative RAI cycles have driven multi-year reviews on several programs, a point flagged in COMSTAC and 2025 CRS reporting (Congressional Research Service R48582).

entries.short_definition: FAA rulemaking effort to revise 14 CFR Part 450, launched in response to the August 2025 SpARC final report and Executive Order 14335 of August 13, 2025.
entries.full_definition:
FAA SLR-2 refers to the second-generation Streamlined Launch and Reentry Licensing rulemaking, an effort by the FAA Office of Commercial Space Transportation (AST) to amend 14 CFR Part 450 after sustained industry criticism that the original SLR-2 final rule, published in the Federal Register on December 10, 2020 and effective March 10, 2021, had increased rather than reduced licensing burden. The revision was triggered by two near-simultaneous actions in August 2025: the final report of the Part 450 Aerospace Rulemaking Committee (the Launch and Reentry License Requirements Improvement SpARC) chartered by the Department of Transportation on November 7, 2024, and Executive Order 14335, Enabling Competition in the Commercial Space Industry, signed August 13, 2025.

The Part 450 SpARC was a 24-member federal advisory committee drawn from launch and reentry operators, spaceports, and safety specialists, tasked with identifying and recommending amendments across roughly nine subject areas, including flight safety analysis under Subpart C, system safety under Subpart D, means-of-compliance procedures, the pre-application consultation process under section 450.33, and the use of mission rules and safety-critical system requirements. Its final report, delivered to AST in August 2025, provides the technical baseline for the rulemaking now generally referred to inside the agency and industry as SLR-2 to distinguish it from the 2020 SLR rule it will supersede.

Executive Order 14335 layered binding policy direction on top of the SpARC record. Section 3(b) directs the Secretary of Transportation to reevaluate, amend, or rescind Part 450 as appropriate, with specific attention to flight termination and safety system requirements, airworthiness treatment of hybrid vehicles that operate as aircraft for portions of flight, reliability conditions imposed on reentry vehicles, and any requirements deemed too attenuated from public safety to justify retention. The Secretary was required to report planned actions to the Assistant to the President for Economic Policy within 120 days, and to use all available authorities, including NEPA categorical exclusions, to eliminate or expedite environmental reviews not required by law.

SLR-2 thus operates on three converging tracks: a notice-and-comment Part 450 amendment informed by the SpARC record, parallel revisions to advisory circulars including AC 450.41-1 on flight safety analysis methodology, and an environmental review reform package executed under EO 14335. The proceeding is the central regulatory event for the US commercial launch sector through the 2030 cadence-growth horizon set by the executive order.

entries.short_definition: FCC 22-74 is the Second Report and Order adopted September 29, 2022 that shortened the post-mission disposal benchmark for LEO satellites from 25 years to 5 years.
entries.full_definition:
FCC 22-74, formally the Second Report and Order in IB Docket Nos. 18-313 and 22-271 (Mitigation of Orbital Debris in the New Space Age), was adopted by the Federal Communications Commission on September 29, 2022 and released the following day. It replaced the long-standing 25-year post-mission disposal guideline, which traced back to NASA and inter-agency debris standards of the 1990s, with a binding 5-year benchmark for any space station authorized by the Commission to operate in or pass through the low Earth orbit region below 2,000 km altitude. Operators must complete disposal as soon as practicable, and no later than five years after end of mission, either through controlled or uncontrolled atmospheric re-entry or by maneuvering to an orbit from which natural decay occurs within that window.

The order amended four provisions of 47 CFR Part 25. Section 25.114(d)(14) was revised to require applicants to demonstrate compliance with the 5-year disposal commitment as part of every space station application. Section 25.121(f) extended the same condition to license terms and renewals, while 25.122(c) and (d) and 25.123(b) imposed parallel obligations on small satellites authorized under streamlined and part-25 processes. The rule reaches both Commission-licensed US operators and non-US-licensed systems granted US market access under the Commission's foreign-licensed satellite procedures; the FCC took the position that any satellite using US-authorized spectrum or communicating with US earth stations falls within scope, regardless of where it is licensed.

The compliance trigger was tied to a two-year transition. Applications filed before September 29, 2024 remained eligible for grandfathering under the prior 25-year practice, including space stations already in orbit and those authorized but not yet launched. Applications filed on or after September 29, 2024, and any post-grant modification that materially alters the mission, must satisfy the 5-year rule outright. The Federal Register notice publishing the codified amendments, Space Innovation; Mitigation of Orbital Debris in the New Space Age, appeared at 89 FR on August 9, 2024 with the rule taking effect September 9, 2024 and the substantive compliance obligation activating on September 29, 2024.

FCC 22-74 is the most consequential US debris regulation in three decades. It put a binding federal rule on top of what had previously been voluntary guidance and now sets the de facto floor that constellation operators, including non-US systems serving the US market, must engineer their propulsion budgets, drag devices, and altitude selection against.

entries.short_definition: 47 CFR Part 25 is the FCC rule set that licenses commercial satellite communications, covering U.S. space stations, earth stations, and non-U.S. market access requests.
entries.full_definition:
47 CFR Part 25, titled Satellite Communications, is the body of Federal Communications Commission rules that governs commercial satellite radio operations involving U.S. jurisdiction. Issued under the Communications Act of 1934, it is organized into Subpart A (general provisions, sections 25.101 to 25.109), Subpart B (applications and licenses, 25.110 to 25.173), Subpart C (technical standards, 25.201 to 25.265), Subpart D (technical operations, 25.271 to 25.290), and Subpart E (miscellaneous, section 25.301). Filings are made through the International Communications Filing System (ICFS) on FCC Form 312, with Form 312EZ used for routine earth stations (per ecfr.gov).

Part 25 distinguishes two regulated facility classes. A space station license authorizes the satellite itself, including its orbital regime, frequencies, and EIRP, while an earth station license authorizes the ground transmitter that communicates with it. The licensing regimes are different: a space station application triggers a 30-day public notice comment period before grant, whereas a Form 312EZ earth station application is deemed granted 35 days after public notice if unopposed (per fcc.gov). Section 25.137 provides a separate path called market access, by which a non-U.S. licensed satellite may serve U.S. earth stations without a domestic Part 25 space station license; WTO-member systems serving services covered by the Basic Telecommunications Agreement are exempt from the effective-competitive-opportunities showing, but must still demonstrate compliance with FCC milestones, service rules, and bond requirements.

Section 25.114 sets out what every space station application must contain, including the orbital debris mitigation disclosure required under 25.114(d)(14). Applicants must address operational debris release, the probability of becoming a debris source through collision with small debris and meteoroids (a 0.01, or 1-in-100, threshold), accidental explosion risk from residual propellant or pressurized systems, and large-object collision risk; NGSO operators must show a probability under 0.001 of collision with objects 10 cm and larger across the mission, and disposal reliability of at least 0.9 (per ecfr.gov).

In the Second Report and Order FCC 22-74, adopted September 29, 2022, the Commission shortened the long-standing 25-year post-mission disposal benchmark for low-Earth-orbit space stations to five years. Codified across 25.114, 25.121, 25.122, and 25.123, the rule applies to FCC-licensed satellites and to market-access grantees launched after September 29, 2024; satellites already in orbit on that date are grandfathered (per fcc.gov and Federal Register notice of August 9, 2024).

entries.short_definition: The FCC process under 47 CFR 25.137 by which a non-U.S. licensed satellite system obtains authority to provide service to or from earth stations located in the United States.
entries.full_definition:
FCC market access is the regulatory mechanism, codified at 47 CFR 25.137, by which an operator whose space station is licensed by a foreign administration may serve the U.S. market without holding an FCC space station license itself. The Commission grants that authority either by acting on an earth station application that designates a non-U.S. licensed satellite as a point of communication, or by issuing a petition for declaratory ruling that adds the foreign system to the FCC's Permitted Space Station List. Filings are submitted electronically through the International Communications Filing System (ICFS), the successor to IBFS, and must include the same legal and technical exhibits that sections 25.114, 25.122, 25.123, or 25.125 would require for a U.S. space station license, including the information needed to complete Schedule S.

The substantive framework flows from the 1997 DISCO II Order. For satellites licensed by a member of the World Trade Organization that will provide services covered by the WTO Basic Telecommunications Agreement, the Commission applies a rebuttable presumption that grant serves the public interest, evaluating spectrum availability, competitive effects, eligibility, and operating requirements rather than reciprocity. Section 25.137(a)(2) preserves an effective competitive opportunities (ECO) test for non-WTO members and non-covered services: the petitioner bears the burden of showing no practical or legal constraints prevent analogous access by U.S.-licensed systems in the licensing country and in every country where traffic will originate or terminate.

Processing follows the same queues as domestic applications. Under 25.137(c), NGSO-like requests are considered contemporaneously with U.S. NGSO applications pursuant to the 25.157 processing round procedure, while GSO-like requests enter the first-come, first-served queue under 25.158. To take a queue position, the petitioner must show that the space station is in orbit and operating, has been licensed by another administration, or has been submitted to the ITU for coordination under the Radio Regulations. Section 25.137(d) then ties the grant to ongoing compliance with FCC milestones, reporting, service rules, and, for systems not yet operational, the surety bond requirement of 25.165, currently 1 million dollars for GSO and 5 million dollars for NGSO systems, plus the six-year build-out milestone.

A market access grant is co-terminous with the underlying foreign license and confers no independent ITU priority; the home administration retains that priority through the ITU API and CRC process. Amendments, modifications, and changes of control must be reported to the Commission, typically within 30 days, under 25.137(e) through (g). Recent constellations including OneWeb, Telesat Lightspeed, and Kepler have entered the U.S. market through 25.137 declaratory rulings rather than direct FCC licensing.

entries.short_definition: IN-SPACe NGP 2024 is the May 2024 norms document operationalising the Indian Space Policy 2023 by setting authorisation procedures for six categories of space activities by Indian entities.
entries.full_definition:
The Norms, Guidelines and Procedures for Implementation of Indian Space Policy 2023 in respect of Authorisation of Space Activities, bearing document number IN:ISP2023:NGP2024/V1.0 and dated 3 May 2024, is the implementation framework issued by the Indian National Space Promotion and Authorisation Centre (IN-SPACe). It translates the high-level Indian Space Policy 2023 into a workable authorisation regime: a single consolidated document listing the activities that require prior approval, who may apply, what must be submitted, and how IN-SPACe will evaluate, condition, and renew authorisations. The NGP is published on inspace.gov.in and is administered through the IN-SPACe Digital Platform (IDP), which serves as the single online portal for applications.

The NGP enumerates broad categories of authorisable space activities, generally summarised as six groupings: establishment and operation of space objects and satellite systems (GSO and NGSO communication, earth observation, hosted payloads); operation of space transportation systems including sub-orbital and orbital launches and planned re-entry; establishment and operation of ground systems such as satellite control centres, TT&C, and data reception stations; dissemination of satellite remote sensing data, including high-resolution earth observation imagery; amateur satellite services; and registration of space objects in the national registry pursuant to UN obligations. End-user satellite services, ground-based testing, and pure hardware manufacturing are clarified as not requiring IN-SPACe authorisation.

Only an Indian Entity, defined as an entity incorporated or registered under Indian law with effective management in India, may apply, and applications are expected at least six months in advance. The NGP integrates with two adjacent regulators rather than displacing them: applicants must declare or obtain International Telecommunication Union (ITU) filing status for TT&C and payload frequencies through the Wireless Planning and Coordination (WPC) Wing of the Department of Telecommunications, which remains the Indian administration for ITU coordination and domestic spectrum licensing. IN-SPACe consumes those filings as part of its evaluation but does not itself allocate spectrum.

IN-SPACe acts as the single-window authoriser for the whole regime, assessing national security, public safety, foreign-relations, and liability-insurance conditions, and is empowered to grant, condition, amend, suspend, or cancel authorisations; the NGP indicates an end-to-end processing window of approximately 75 to 120 days from a complete application. Authorisations are non-transferable without written IN-SPACe approval, and changes of control must be reported within 48 hours, as summarised by JSA and Khaitan in their 2024 analyses of the inspace.gov.in notification.

entries.short_definition: ITU Radio Regulations procedure under Articles 9 and 11 in which an operator files Advance Publication Information then a Coordination Request, securing a frequency assignment within a 7-year deadline.
entries.full_definition:
The Advance Publication Information (API) and Coordination Request (CR/C) are the two foundational filings an administration submits to the ITU Radiocommunication Bureau to acquire and protect satellite frequency assignments under the Radio Regulations. API is governed by Section I of Article 9 (Nos. 9.1 and 9.2): the administration sends the Bureau a general description of a planned satellite network or system, drawn from Appendix 4, not earlier than seven years and preferably not later than two years before the planned date of bringing into use. The Bureau publishes the submission as an API/A Special Section in the BR International Frequency Information Circular (BR IFIC) within three months, opening a four-month comment window that may yield an API/B publication of comments.

For frequency bands subject to coordination under Nos. 9.7 to 9.14 and 9.21, the administration must then file a Coordination Request under Section II of Article 9, published by the Bureau as a CR/C Special Section in the BR IFIC. Under No. 9.1A, receipt of the CR/C automatically triggers publication of an API/C and starts the regulatory time limit. The CR/C identifies all administrations whose existing or planned networks fall within the coordination trigger (for example, the 6 degree GSO arc for conventional FSS bands or 8 degrees in Q/V band), and those administrations have four months to respond under Nos. 9.11 to 9.14 and 9.21 before being deemed unaffected.

The seven-year regulatory deadline is set by No. 11.44 in conjunction with No. 9.1, and is enforced through Article 11 notification. The frequency assignment must be brought into use within seven years of the date the Bureau receives the complete API or CR/C information, or the assignment is cancelled. For geostationary networks, bringing into use under No. 11.44B requires a space station with the notified frequencies to be deployed and maintained at the notified orbital position for a continuous 90-day period. For non-geostationary systems, Resolution 35 (WRC-19) imposes milestone-based deployment requirements at 2, 5, and 7 years after the end of the regulatory period, tied to the share of the constellation actually launched.

Once the assignment is brought into use and notified under Article 11, the Bureau examines it and, on a favourable finding, records it in the Master International Frequency Register (MIFR) via a Part II-S publication, conferring international recognition and protection from harmful interference. Failure to meet any procedural deadline, including the 24-month window under No. 9.30 between API and CR/C, results in the filing being suppressed from the Bureau's records.

entries.short_definition: ITU procedure requiring an administration to file identity data on the satellite network operator, spacecraft manufacturer, and launch services provider within the seven-year bringing-into-use period.
entries.full_definition:
Resolution 49 (Rev. WRC-19) is the administrative due diligence procedure of the ITU Radio Regulations, applicable to satellite networks and systems subject to coordination under No. 9.1A and to assignments in the Plans of Appendices 30, 30A, and 30B. It was first adopted at WRC-97 to curb "paper satellites" and has been revised at successive World Radiocommunication Conferences; the current text was approved by WRC-19 as part of the Final Acts signed in Sharm el-Sheikh on 22 November 2019.

Under resolves 1 and the format prescribed in Annex 2, the notifying administration must submit a single set of identity information for each frequency assignment subject to the procedure. The data are organised in three parts. Part A, identity of the satellite network, gives the network name, the name of the operator, the orbital position or non-geostationary orbital parameters, the frequency bands, and the responsible administration. Part B, spacecraft manufacturer, requires the name of the manufacturer, the date of execution of the procurement contract, the contractual delivery window (beginning and end dates), and the number of satellites procured. Part C, launch services provider, requires the name of the provider, the date of execution of the launch contract, the name of the launch vehicle, the name of the launching facility, and the contractual launch or in-orbit delivery window. Items B and C may be reported as in-house procurement where the administration, operator, manufacturer, or launcher are the same entity.

Timing is fixed by the bringing-into-use clock in No. 11.44, which gives an administration seven years from receipt of the advance publication or coordination request to bring assignments into regular operation. Resolution 49 information must reach the Radiocommunication Bureau as early as possible, and in any case no later than 30 days after the end of that seven-year period; for Appendix 30B Part A submissions an interim deadline tied to Article 6 applies. Filings are transmitted electronically using SpaceCap Version 9.0.0 or later, with the RS49 module generating the form from the underlying Article 4 or Article 6 notice.

The consequence of non-submission is severe and self-executing: under resolves 4 the Bureau cancels the frequency assignments, the entry is removed from the Master International Frequency Register or the relevant Plan, and the notifying administration is informed. WRC-19 also realigned the resolves with the No. 9.1A timetable and suppressed transition measures that had expired, leaving Resolution 49 as the principal milestone gate between a filed network and a real spacecraft on contract.

entries.short_definition: License Exception Commercial Space Activities (CSA) is a Bureau of Industry and Security authorization, proposed in October 2024, allowing license-free exports of specified space items to close allies and U.S. persons.
entries.full_definition:
License Exception CSA (Commercial Space Activities) is a new authorization under the Export Administration Regulations developed by the Department of Commerce Bureau of Industry and Security (BIS) and published as a proposed rule on October 23, 2024 at 89 FR 84784. CSA was introduced as one of three coordinated "Space" rulemakings issued that day alongside the Space One final rule at 89 FR 84766 and the Space Two interim final rule at 89 FR 84758. It is intended to be codified at 15 CFR 740.27 (proposed during a period when section 740.26 was also under consideration) and to function as the principal license-free pathway for routine commercial space cooperation with trusted partners. Comments were originally due November 22, 2024 and were later extended by 30 days at 89 FR 91295.

The exception provides two authorizations. The first covers "official space agency programs," permitting export, reexport, and transfer (in-country) of items strictly within the scope of designated cooperative programs such as Artemis, the Lunar Gateway, and Mars Sample Return. The second covers manned spacecraft, including parts, components, accessories, and attachments, used in space tourism and fundamental research missions, subject to four conditions: suborbital trajectory only, no transfer of registration or ownership to a foreign person, FAA (or foreign equivalent) approval of destinations, and no destinations in Country Groups D:5 or E. Eligible end users are limited to U.S. persons and entities in Country Group A:5 (Wassenaar participating states) and A:6 (additional close partners), with consignment confined to the same destinations.

CSA excludes a defined set of higher-risk items and transactions. It does not authorize exports to proscribed persons under Part 744, transactions subject to end-use or end-user controls, offshore procurement arrangements, exports to Country Groups D:1, D:4, or D:5, or radiation-hardened spacecraft components classified in ECCN 9A515.d or 9A515.e. The exception also disclaims use for items already controlled for missile technology or chemical and biological reasons that fall outside its enumerated ECCNs.

Exporters using CSA must comply with the standard Part 762 recordkeeping requirements, retain records for five years, file Electronic Export Information citing the CSA license code and the appropriate ECCN per 15 CFR 740.1 and 758.1, and apply the Destination Control Statement at 758.6. The exception is designed to operate in tandem with the Department of State's parallel U.S. Munitions List Category XV revisions and the new DDTC exemptions for space agency, launch support, and space tourism activities, narrowing licensing burdens on allied commercial space supply chains while preserving controls on the most sensitive hardware.

entries.short_definition: MIL-STD-461 is the U.S. Department of Defense interface standard governing electromagnetic interference emission and susceptibility characteristics of electronic subsystems and equipment.
entries.full_definition:
MIL-STD-461 establishes interface and verification requirements for the control of electromagnetic interference (EMI) emission and susceptibility characteristics of electronic, electrical, and electromechanical equipment and subsystems procured by the U.S. Department of Defense. The current revision, MIL-STD-461G, was released on 11 December 2015 and supersedes MIL-STD-461F (10 December 2007). Per the scope section, the standard applies to equipment with enclosures no larger than equipment racks, including discrete wiring harnesses between enclosures, and is not intended to be applied directly to modules inside enclosures or to entire platforms (everyspec.com).

The standard is organized into four families of test methods. Conducted Emissions (CE) covers EMI traveling along power and signal lines: CE101 addresses audio-frequency currents on power leads from 30 Hz to 10 kHz, CE102 addresses RF potentials on power leads from 10 kHz to 10 MHz, and CE106 addresses antenna-port emissions. Conducted Susceptibility (CS) injects disturbances onto leads and includes CS101 (power-lead ripple), CS114 (bulk cable injection), CS115 (impulse excitation), CS116 (damped sinusoidal transients), and the two methods new in revision G: CS117 for lightning indirect effects and CS118 for personnel electrostatic discharge; CS106 from revision F was removed in G (In Compliance Magazine; Interference Technology). Radiated Emissions (RE) include RE101 (magnetic field, 30 Hz to 100 kHz), RE102 (electric field, 10 kHz to 18 GHz), and RE103 (antenna spurious and harmonics). Radiated Susceptibility (RS) includes RS101 (magnetic field) and RS103 (electric field, typically 2 MHz to 18 GHz, extendable to 40 GHz).

For commercial space, MIL-STD-461 is not legally required outside DoD procurement, but launch service providers and payload integrators routinely flow it down because government and rideshare missions invoke it and because no equivalent civil standard exists with the same depth. The standard itself does not define a "space" platform column equivalent to Army, Navy, and Air Force entries; applicability for launch payloads is therefore tailored mission by mission, often through an interface control document or by combining MIL-STD-461 method limits with NASA GEVS (GSFC-STD-7000) workmanship envelopes. Typical launch-vehicle interface control documents call out CE101/CE102, RE102, RS103, and CS114/CS115/CS116 as the baseline for payloads, with CS117 added when lightning indirect effects on the pad or in transit are credible. The full standard is available as a 3.17 MB PDF from everyspec.com and from the Defense Standardization Program ASSIST database.

entries.short_definition: NASA Goddard's General Environmental Verification Standard (GSFC-STD-7000), the agency baseline that defines test levels and methods for qualifying spacecraft hardware to mission environments.
entries.full_definition:
NASA GEVS is the General Environmental Verification Standard, formally designated GSFC-STD-7000 and issued by Goddard Space Flight Center. The current edition, Revision B, was approved on 28 April 2021 and is on a five-year review cycle with the next review due 28 April 2027. It provides requirements and guidelines for environmental verification of GSFC payloads, subsystems, and components, and it describes acceptable test and analytical methods for demonstrating performance in the launch and on-orbit environments and for confirming workmanship (standards.nasa.gov).

GEVS is organized around the major environments encountered by an uncrewed spacecraft: structural and mechanical loads, acoustics, pyroshock, sine and random vibration, thermal-vacuum and thermal-balance, humidity, electromagnetic compatibility, contamination control, and end-to-end mission verification. The standard distinguishes protoflight, qualification, and acceptance test categories and publishes generalized minimum levels intended as a starting point when project-specific environments are not yet defined. The most widely cited example is the generalized random vibration profile, which yields an overall level of 14.1 Grms for protoflight or qualification at the component level and a 6.8 Grms workmanship screen for acceptance, applied for one minute per axis (GEVS Section 2.4; see also the NASA NTRS workmanship discussion). Section 2.5 covers EMC and tailors MIL-STD-461 limits to typical GSFC missions, assuming a metal-chassis spacecraft under roughly 2 m per side on a 28 Vdc bus; Revision B expanded Section 2.5.3 with rationale and added common-mode conducted-emissions testing using current probes and absorbing clamps up to 200 MHz (EMC United).

GEVS is the NASA civil-space counterpart to the U.S. Air Force / Space Force test family MIL-STD-1540 and its successor SMC-S-016 / Aerospace TR-2004(8583), which serve the national-security launch and satellite community. The two documents are technically aligned in philosophy (protoflight margins, qualification factors, acceptance screens) but differ in tailoring assumptions and contractual flow-down. Neither is mandatory outside its home community, but rideshare manifests and launch services often invoke GEVS levels by reference when project-specific environments are immature.

Smallsat and CubeSat developers commonly cite GEVS as their environmental baseline, especially for NASA-funded missions and university payloads. Revision B added Appendix B specifically addressing environmental verification for CubeSats as a companion to the main standard, acknowledging the rideshare context and tighter mass and schedule constraints. Programs typically use GEVS levels until launch-vehicle-specific coupled-loads analyses and dispenser interface control documents produce project-specific environments, at which point GEVS becomes the fallback workmanship and screening reference (standards.nasa.gov).

entries.short_definition: A NOAA licensing regime under 15 CFR Part 960 that authorizes private Earth remote sensing space systems and sorts them into three tiers based on data availability.
entries.full_definition:
NOAA Commercial Remote Sensing Licensing, administered by the Commercial Remote Sensing Regulatory Affairs (CRSRA) office within NESDIS and the Office of Space Commerce, is the U.S. licensing program for private Earth remote sensing space systems. Statutory authority comes from the Land Remote Sensing Policy Act of 1992, codified at 51 U.S.C. 60101 et seq., and the implementing rules are at 15 CFR Part 960. The license is required for any U.S. person operating a private remote sensing space system, and for foreign-owned systems with sufficient U.S. nexus such as U.S. launch or U.S. ground station use. Per 15 CFR 960.3, the regulations apply only to in-orbit remote sensing of Earth that can be processed into imagery of surface features, so they do not capture all forms of space-based sensing.

The current framework comes from the Final Rule published in the Federal Register on May 20, 2020 (85 FR 30790), which took effect July 20, 2020 and replaced the 2006 regulations in their entirety. 15 CFR 960.6 categorizes each application into one of three tiers based on a comparison to unenhanced data already available from other sources. Tier 1 covers systems whose unenhanced data is substantially the same as data already available from entities not licensed under Part 960 (typically foreign operators or unlicensed U.S. sources). Tier 2 covers systems substantially the same as data available only from other Part 960 licensees. Tier 3 covers systems with capabilities not substantially the same as any domestic or foreign source, that is, novel capabilities.

Operating conditions scale with tier. Tier 1 systems face only the minimal conditions in 15 CFR 960.8. Tier 2 and Tier 3 systems are governed by 960.9 and 960.10 respectively, but the 2020 reform sharply reduced the prior practice of imposing capability-specific restrictions on synthetic aperture radar, night-time imaging, short-wave infrared, and non-Earth imaging. Per the Federal Register notice, any temporary operating conditions on truly unique Tier 3 capabilities may last no more than three years unless the Secretary of Defense or Secretary of State personally requests and justifies an extension, shifting more of the mitigation burden onto the government. The application path runs through an Initial Contact Form, a 7-day completeness review by interagency partners, and a statutory determination period of up to 60 days, per the Office of Space Commerce licensing page. CRSRA periodically re-tiers systems as foreign benchmarks evolve, as shown in the quarterly Foreign Benchmarks reports.

entries.short_definition: A three-tier U.S. commercial remote-sensing licensing framework under 15 CFR Part 960 (effective July 20, 2020) that scales license conditions based on whether comparable unenhanced data is available from foreign or domestic sources.
entries.full_definition:
The Tier 1, 2, and 3 framework is the categorization scheme NOAA's Commercial Remote Sensing Regulatory Affairs (CRSRA) office uses to license private remote-sensing space systems under 15 CFR Part 960, the final rule published in the Federal Register on May 20, 2020 and effective July 20, 2020. The rule replaced the previous one-size-fits-all conditions regime with a graduated structure in which the burden on the licensee tracks the availability of substantially similar unenhanced data elsewhere in the world. Tier assignment is made by the Secretary of Commerce under 15 CFR 960.6 based on the Data Availability Notification Form submitted with the application.

Tier 1, governed by 15 CFR 960.8, covers systems whose unenhanced data is substantially the same as data already available from entities not licensed under Part 960, such as foreign operators. It is the fastest and least restrictive category: licensees receive only those conditions required by statute, and prior limited-operations directives (so-called shutter control), along with the legacy operating restrictions on synthetic aperture radar, night-time imaging, short-wave infrared, and non-Earth imaging, are not automatically imposed. Tier 2, under 15 CFR 960.9, applies when comparable data is available only from other Part 960 licensees; these systems receive the Tier 1 conditions plus a defined set of additional standard conditions reflecting the lack of a foreign benchmark.

Tier 3, under 15 CFR 960.10, applies to systems proposing to collect novel unenhanced data not substantially available from any domestic or foreign source. Tier 3 licensees receive the Tier 1 and Tier 2 conditions and may receive additional standard and temporary license conditions that NOAA, in consultation with the Departments of Defense and State, crafts to be the least restrictive necessary to address a specific and compelling national-security or foreign-policy risk; tier reassignment to a lower-numbered tier follows once foreign or domestic competitors emerge.

Application timelines are codified in Subpart B: NOAA conducts a completeness review of no more than 7 days, and once an application is deemed complete the agency must issue a licensing determination within 60 days, making the U.S. commercial remote-sensing regime one of the most predictable in the world. Tier assignments may be revisited at any time on the Secretary's initiative or at the licensee's request as the global data-availability landscape evolves.

entries.short_definition: Thermal vacuum (TVAC) is an environmental test that exposes spacecraft hardware to high vacuum and cyclic hot and cold extremes to verify thermal design, workmanship, and functional performance.
entries.full_definition:
Thermal vacuum (TVAC) testing places flight hardware inside a vacuum chamber pumped below roughly 1e-5 Torr while shrouds drive the article through hot and cold temperature extremes that bound the on-orbit environment. The test simultaneously verifies that the thermal design works as predicted in the absence of convection and that workmanship defects, which thermal cycling tends to expose, are caught on the ground rather than in flight. Per NASA's General Environmental Verification Standard (GSFC-STD-7000, the GEVS), TVAC is required at multiple levels of assembly, with chamber pressure held below 1.33e-3 Pa (about 1e-5 Torr) and temperature extremes set a margin beyond the worst-case predicted hot and cold flight temperatures.

TVAC programs distinguish two complementary activities. Thermal balance demonstrates the thermal control system and correlates the thermal math model: the article is held at stable hot and cold plateaus until temperatures converge, and the recorded heater powers and gradients are used to anchor the model. Thermal cycling, by contrast, drives repeated transitions between hot and cold extremes to surface latent workmanship defects and verify functional performance across the operating envelope. GEVS calls for protoflight margins of +/-10 deg C beyond worst-case predictions, with cycle counts and dwells that scale with assembly level (for example, on the order of eight cycles at unit level and four at observatory level, with abbreviated functional tests at each plateau and full functional tests at hot and cold soak). A common protoflight rule of thumb is eight to twelve thermal cycles, with the larger number applied to lower assembly levels per GEVS Section 2.4 / 2.6.

A bakeout phase typically precedes or is folded into TVAC to drive off adsorbed water and condensable volatiles before contamination-sensitive surfaces are exposed. Bakeout temperatures and durations are selected against ASTM E595, which screens spacecraft polymers by exposing samples at 125 deg C and pressure below 7e-3 Pa (5e-5 Torr) for 24 hours and measuring total mass loss (TML) and collected volatile condensable materials (CVCM). The widely used screening thresholds, codified in GEVS and the NASA Outgassing Database, are TML at most 1.00 percent and CVCM at most 0.10 percent; materials that fail are either replaced or pre-baked until they meet the limits. For smallsats and CubeSats, programs commonly tailor GEVS to four to eight cycles with dwell times of two to six hours and a +/-10 deg C protoflight margin, while keeping the ASTM E595 outgassing limits unchanged because contamination physics does not scale with spacecraft size.

entries.short_definition: USML Category XV is the ITAR control list at 22 CFR 121.1 covering spacecraft and related articles whose export is licensed by the State Department's DDTC.
entries.full_definition:
USML Category XV (Spacecraft and Related Articles) is the section of the United States Munitions List, codified at 22 CFR 121.1, that designates spacecraft and directly related technical data and defense services as ITAR-controlled defense articles. Items in this category are subject to licensing by the State Department's Directorate of Defense Trade Controls (DDTC, at pmddtc.state.gov), rather than by the Commerce Department's Bureau of Industry and Security. The category is organized into paragraphs that enumerate end-items, ground control equipment, parts and components, and supporting technical data, with paragraphs (a) through (e) listing articles and the technical data paragraph capturing information directly related to them. Export, reexport, and brokering of Cat XV items require ITAR authorization under 22 CFR Parts 123, 124, and 125.

Following Section 1261 of the FY2013 NDAA, which repealed the blanket statutory requirement (originating in the 1999 Strom Thurmond NDAA, Section 1513) that all satellites be ITAR-controlled, State and Commerce issued companion rules on May 13, 2014 to restructure Category XV. The State Department's interim final rule (79 FR 27180) became effective November 10, 2014, with paragraph (d) on radiation-hardened microelectronics effective June 27, 2014; a follow-on final rule (82 FR 2889) took effect January 15, 2017. After reform, Cat XV retains spacecraft and components specially designed for military, intelligence, or otherwise sensitive missions, including items such as MILSATCOM with anti-jam features, certain remote sensing satellites that exceed performance thresholds enumerated in paragraph (a), classified spacecraft, and specified radiation-hardened microelectronics in paragraph (d). Commercial communications, scientific, and most civil remote sensing satellites no longer warranting USML control were transferred to the Commerce Control List under the new 9x515 ECCNs (9A515, 9B515, 9C515, 9D515, 9E515) at 15 CFR 774.

Defense services tied to Cat XV items are controlled under the ITAR definition at 22 CFR 120.32 (formerly 120.9) and typically require a Technical Assistance Agreement under 22 CFR Part 124 before a U.S. person may furnish assistance to a foreign person in the design, development, integration, launch, or operation of a covered spacecraft. The 9x515 ECCNs sit alongside Cat XV in the order of review at Supplement No. 4 to 15 CFR Part 774: if an item is described in Cat XV it is ITAR-controlled, and only items outside Cat XV are evaluated against 9x515 and the rest of the CCL. The 2014 reforms preserved the principle that classified technical data directly related to Cat XV items, and defense services using that classified data, remain ITAR-controlled regardless of where the hardware itself is classified.
